Front Page Dashboard: Hypothesis, Evidence, and Citation Audit

This first screen is the presentation dashboard. It summarizes the search-before-generation invariant, the generated hypothesis portfolio, the evidence depth behind the quality audit, the claim/citation audit table, and the exact places to inspect in the detailed report. External chat delivery is treated only as an optional wrapper, not as part of this dashboard's navigation or evidence logic.

Evidence chainEvery major conclusion should point to evidence IDs or an explicit inferred status.

System Architecture

1. InputPDF path, arXiv ID, URL, and optional research guidance.
2. GroundingPDF parsing, reference cleaning, related-paper search, and evidence extraction.
3. Idea AgentLiterature map, gap/tension synthesis, hypothesis portfolio, and novelty search.
4. AuditReference identity, claim support, Section 6 evidence coverage, evidence cards, and memory.

Hypothesis Evidence Depth

  • Section 6 rows: 16 component-level audit row(s).
  • Precision evidence per component: min 0, max 0, avg 0.
  • Status mix: needs human review 9evidence inspired 6model inferred 1
  • Interpretation: evidence bundles are capped and relevance-ranked; unsupported bridges are labeled as inference instead of padded with weak citations.

Generated Hypothesis Portfolio

H1 primary score 0.67; inference inferred
The neural tangent kernel (NTK) of an implicit model changes with the number of test-time fixed-point iterations, contradicting the infinite-width NTK theory that predicts a fixed kernel.
H2 secondary score 0.505; inference inferred
Generalization bounds for implicit models that require contractivity can be extended to non-contractive regular operators by incorporating a Lipschitz constant that grows with the number of test-time iterations.
Both selected hypotheses are shown on the front page; treat new ideas as audit targets, not proven facts.

Review Walkthrough

  • 1. Provide input: submit a paper PDF, paper URL, arXiv ID, or research guidance through the CLI or an optional chat wrapper.
  • 2. Run agent: the pipeline records every tool step and stores reusable memory.
  • 3. Open this page: explain the dashboard before drilling into details.
  • 4. Show audit logic: GREEN is direct support, YELLOW is partial/inferred, RED is failure or contradiction.

Review Warnings

1. Run Summary

This section is the executive checksum for the run. It records the run ID, verifies the most important ordering constraint, and defines the report's audit colors. The key process guarantee is that literature search must occur before hypothesis generation, so the generated idea is grounded in retrieved papers rather than produced from the input PDF alone. Read GREEN/YELLOW/RED as claim-audit labels, not as paper-quality labels.

Run ID: 33c6b43c-4ca0-43df-809d-c21cee2f9013

Literature search happened before hypothesis generation: YES.

GREEN labels require verified references, bibliographic score >= 0.75, direct support, evidence snippets, provenance, and a written reason. The score column in the verification table is bibliographic identity confidence, not support strength. YELLOW means uncertain, partial, or a novel claim that still needs experiment; RED means not found, unsupported, contradicted, or clearly wrong.

Direct evidenceReference identity is verified, evidence explicitly addresses the claim, and the reason explains the support relation.
Indirect evidenceEvidence is relevant background or motivation, but it does not directly prove the full claim.
Background / absentEvidence is too weak, abstract-only, missing, or only useful as context.
Contradictory evidenceEvidence conflicts with the claim or contains explicit contradiction cues.

Four Error Categories

Unsupported ClaimThe conclusion is not supported by any available input material.
OverclaimThe material supports a weaker conclusion, but the generated claim states a stronger one.
Mis-citationThe cited material is topic-related but does not support the specific claim.
ContradictionThe generated claim conflicts with the cited material.

Evidence Source Snapshot

Total evidence records: 42; input-paper evidence: 7; retrieved/external evidence: 35; abstract evidence ratio: 0.667.

2. Input Paper Profiles

This section shows the structured profile extracted from each submitted PDF before any external retrieval or hypothesis generation. A profile contains the detected title, authors, abstract, and high-level source claims. These fields are produced by deterministic PDF parsing and profiling heuristics, with optional LLM assistance only when enabled. They are not treated as final truth: if the title, abstract, author list, or key claims are wrong, later search queries, relatedness filtering, literature-map items, and citation audits should be interpreted cautiously.

Clean profileTitle, authors, abstract, and key claims match the PDF; downstream retrieval is likely aligned.
Partial profileSome fields are missing or noisy; search may still work, but map and hypothesis quality should be reviewed manually.
Bad profileTitle/abstract/key claims are visibly wrong; later sections may be answering the wrong paper and should be treated as unreliable.

EXPRESSIVE POWER OF IMPLICIT MODELS:

Authors: Rich Equilibria, Test-Time Scaling, Jialin Liu

Abstract: Implicit models, an emerging model class, compute outputs by iterating a sin- gle parameter block to a fixed point. This architecture realizes an infinite-depth, weight-tied network that trains with constant memory, significantly reducing memory needs for the same level of performance compared to explicit models. While it is empirically known that these compact models can often match or even exceed the accuracy of larger explicit networks by allocating more test-time com- pute, the underlying reasons are not yet well understood. We study this gap through a non-parametric analysis of expressive power. We provide a strict mathematical characterization, showing that a simple and regular implicit operator can, through iteration, progressively express more complex map- pings. We prove that for a broad class of implicit models, this process allows the model’s expressive power to grow with test-time compute, ultimately matching a much richer function class. The theory is validated across four domains: imaging, scientific computing, operations research, and LLM reasoning, demonstrating that as test-time iterations increase, the complexity of the learned mapping rises, while the solution quality simultaneously improves and stabilizes.

Key claims:

  • The theory is validated across four domains: imaging, scientific computing, operations research, and LLM reasoning, demonstrating that as test-time iterations increase, the complexity of the learned mapping rises, while the solution quality simultaneously improves and stabilizes.
  • This architecture realizes an infinite-depth, weight-tied network that trains with constant memory, significantly reducing memory needs for the same level of performance compared to explicit models.
  • While it is empirically known that these compact models can often match or even exceed the accuracy of larger explicit networks by allocating more test-time compute, the underlying reasons are not yet well understood.
  • Implicit models, an emerging model class, compute outputs by iterating a single parameter block to a fixed point.

3. Paper Index Legend

This section defines the shorthand paper labels used throughout the report. Labels such as P1, P2, ... refer to retrieved or connected papers after deduplication and relatedness filtering; the internal JSON index remains zero-based, so P1 corresponds to JSON index 0. The relation field explains why a paper entered the candidate pool, for example input seed, cited by the input paper, citing the input paper, semantic expansion, or topical search. The relatedness score is the gate score used before map and hypothesis generation; it is not a citation-support score.

Distance 0 / input seedThe submitted paper itself; useful as context but not enough to prove a new idea.
Distance 1-2Cited-by or citing-neighborhood papers; these are stronger candidates for connected literature.
Semantic expansionTitle/abstract-near papers added when citation graph is sparse; useful but weaker than citation-chain evidence.
Low relatednessRejected or marginal papers should not support hypotheses, even if they are real papers.

4. Literature Map Summary

This section turns the connected paper pool into a literature map. A thread is a cluster of papers that share a method, problem setting, or conceptual role. A gap is a missing test, boundary condition, mechanism, or application regime suggested by the connected evidence. A tension records an unresolved contrast, such as two papers making claims that are hard to compare directly or using incompatible assumptions. A cross-domain connection is a possible transfer of an idea from one setting to another. These items are generated from paper titles, abstracts, retrieved snippets, and evidence IDs; they are the immediate inputs to hypothesis generation, not final claims by themselves.

Thread onlyThe literature clusters cleanly, but may not yet imply a new research question.
Gap + evidenceA plausible place to generate a hypothesis; check evidence IDs before trusting it.
TensionA stronger idea source when assumptions, benchmarks, or claims do not line up across papers.
Empty tension/connectionNot a failure by itself; it means the retrieved literature did not expose a clear contrast or transfer opportunity.

Map generation mode: llm. Fallback used: False. Reason: LLM returned a valid literature map with at least one thread and one gap.

Threads

  • T1 Expressive power scaling with test-time compute in implicit models: P1 and P2 establish that implicit models can match or exceed explicit model accuracy by allocating more test-time compute, and provide a non-parametric analysis showing that expressive power grows with iterations, ultimately representing locally Lipschitz functions. This is validated across imaging, scientific computing, operations research, and LLM reasoning.
    Evidence: Evidence 6 Evidence 15 Evidence 12 Evidence 19 Evidence 7 Evidence 16
  • T2 Equivalence between implicit DEQs and shallow explicit networks under Gaussian mixtures: P3 (and P6) shows that for high-dimensional Gaussian mixture data, the conjugate kernel and neural tangent kernel of a DEQ depend only on a system of four nonlinear equations, and a shallow explicit network can be designed to produce the same kernel, implying functional equivalence in that setting.
    Evidence: Evidence 20 Evidence 24 Evidence 25
  • T3 Generalization bounds for contractive implicit networks: P4 provides a generalization bound for implicit networks defined by contractive fixed-point operators, using Rademacher complexity and covering number arguments, addressing the under-explored theoretical generalization of implicit models.
    Evidence: Evidence 28 Evidence 30
  • T4 Deterministic NTK of deep equilibrium models: P5 shows that DEQ models have a deterministic NTK even when width and depth go to infinity simultaneously, unlike explicit networks, and this NTK can be computed efficiently via root-finding.
    Evidence: Evidence 31 Evidence 32

Gaps

  • G1: The connection between the expressive power scaling with test-time compute (P1) and the kernel-based equivalence to shallow explicit networks (P3) is not established. P1's non-parametric analysis focuses on function-space expressivity, while P3's kernel analysis suggests a different notion of equivalence. It is unclear whether the scaling of expressive power with iterations translates to a change in the NTK or CK, or whether the kernel remains fixed. Why it matters: Bridging these two perspectives could unify understanding of implicit models: whether test-time compute scaling changes the effective kernel or merely the function class.
    Evidence: Evidence 6 Evidence 20
  • G2: Generalization bounds for implicit networks (P4) are derived for contractive operators, but P1's theory applies to a broader class of 'regular' implicit operators that may not be contractive. The generalization behavior of non-contractive implicit models is unknown. Why it matters: Many practical implicit models may not be contractive; understanding their generalization is crucial for reliable deployment.
    Evidence: Evidence 5 Evidence 27
  • G3: P5 shows DEQs have a deterministic NTK, but P1's theory suggests expressive power grows with iterations. It is unknown whether the NTK changes with the number of iterations used at test time, or if it remains fixed regardless of iteration depth. Why it matters: If the NTK is fixed, then the expressive power scaling must arise from non-kernel aspects of the model, challenging the NTK-centric view of deep learning theory.
    Evidence: Evidence 6 Evidence 31
  • G4: P1's theory is validated on four domains, but the theoretical analysis assumes a 'regular' implicit operator. The precise definition of 'regular' and the conditions under which the theory holds (e.g., contractivity, Lipschitzness) are not fully characterized in the provided evidence. Why it matters: Without a clear characterization, it is difficult to apply the theory to new implicit model architectures or to know its limitations.
    Evidence: Evidence 5 Evidence 12

Tensions

  • X1: P1 claims that implicit models can match or exceed explicit model accuracy by using more test-time compute, while P3 suggests that DEQs are almost equivalent to shallow explicit networks in terms of kernel behavior for Gaussian mixtures. This creates a tension: if DEQs are equivalent to shallow explicit networks, how can they outperform deeper explicit networks via test-time compute?
    Evidence: Evidence 3 Evidence 24
  • X2: P4 provides generalization bounds for contractive implicit networks, but P1's theory does not require contractivity and shows that expressive power grows with iterations. If the operator is not contractive, the fixed point may not be unique or the iteration may not converge, conflicting with the assumptions needed for generalization bounds.
    Evidence: Evidence 5 Evidence 27
  • X3: P5 shows DEQs have a deterministic NTK, implying a stable kernel, while P1's empirical results show that the complexity of the learned mapping increases with iterations. If the kernel is fixed, how can the mapping complexity increase? This suggests that either the NTK does not capture the full expressivity, or the empirical complexity measure is not aligned with kernel complexity.
    Evidence: Evidence 7 Evidence 31

Cross-Domain Connections

  • C1: The concept of expressive power scaling with test-time compute in implicit models (P1) can be transferred from the domain of deep learning theory to the domain of scientific computing, where iterative solvers are common. In scientific computing, the number of iterations in a solver (e.g., for PDEs) directly affects solution accuracy, analogous to how iterations in implicit models improve mapping complexity. Rationale: Both domains use iterative processes to refine solutions; the theoretical insight that iterations unlock expressive power could inspire new iterative algorithms in scientific computing that adaptively allocate compute.
    Evidence: Evidence 7
  • C2: The kernel equivalence result from P3 (DEQs equivalent to shallow explicit networks for Gaussian mixtures) can be transferred from the domain of theoretical machine learning to the domain of operations research, where Gaussian mixture models are used for clustering or classification. This could lead to simpler, more interpretable models for operations research problems. Rationale: Operations research often uses complex models; the equivalence suggests that shallow explicit networks can replace DEQs without loss of performance, reducing computational cost.
    Evidence: Evidence 24 Evidence 25

5. Selected Hypothesis Portfolio

This section is the short answer: it presents the selected research-idea portfolio. By default the report keeps up to two hypotheses: a primary proposal and, when available, a distinct secondary or more incremental proposal. Each hypothesis is generated from the literature map and connected evidence, then scored for literature support, novelty, feasibility, cross-domain value, risk, obvious-extension penalty, and prior-work similarity. The primary hypothesis is used for novelty search and citation audit below. The secondary hypothesis is retained as a usable alternative, not as proof that it is equally novel or equally well supported.

Primary + high noveltyBest candidate for a new research direction, subject to novelty-search caveats.
Primary + high riskInteresting but fragile; prioritize feasibility and falsification before presenting it as a project.
Incremental secondaryUsable fallback or extension; expected to be less novel but often easier to test.
No evidence IDsIdea is mostly model inference; it should not be treated as source-supported.

Hypothesis generation mode: llm. Fallback used: False. Reason: LLM returned valid hypothesis candidates after binding and review.
Review: {'used': True, 'reason': 'LLM review returned replacement/refined hypotheses.', 'candidate_count': 2}

primary

H1 - The neural tangent kernel (NTK) of an implicit model changes with the number of test-time fixed-point iterations, contradicting the infinite-width NTK theory that predicts a fixed kernel.

Total score: 0.67 Inference: inferred Novelty: 0.8 Feasibility: 0.7 Obvious-extension penalty: 0.1

Why retained: Primary proposal selected for the main novelty search and citation audit. It is the strongest non-incremental candidate when available; otherwise it is the highest-scoring distinct candidate.

Motivation: P1 shows that the function class expressed by implicit models grows with iterations, while P5 claims the NTK is deterministic and fixed regardless of depth. If the NTK remains fixed, the increased expressivity must come from non-kernel aspects. However, it is possible that the NTK itself changes with iterations when the model is not in the infinite-width limit, or that the fixed-point iteration induces a different kernel. Testing this would bridge the gap between the two perspectives (G1, G3).

Novelty position: This hypothesis challenges the prevailing NTK theory for implicit models by suggesting that the kernel is not static but evolves with test-time compute, which would reconcile the expressivity scaling with kernel-based analyses.

How to test it: Train a DEQ on a regression task. For a fixed set of test inputs, compute the empirical NTK matrix after k iterations (k=1,10,50,100) using the Jacobian of the network output with respect to parameters. Compare the NTK matrices across k; if they differ significantly, the hypothesis is supported.

Premise evidence: Evidence 6 Evidence 31

Supporting papers: P1: EXPRESSIVE POWER OF IMPLICIT MODELS: P5: On the Neural Tangent Kernel of Equilibrium Models

Risks:

  • The NTK may be defined only at the fixed point; intermediate iterations may not correspond to a well-defined kernel.
  • The deterministic NTK result of P5 holds in the infinite-width limit; finite-width effects may dominate.
secondary

H2 - Generalization bounds for implicit models that require contractivity can be extended to non-contractive regular operators by incorporating a Lipschitz constant that grows with the number of test-time iterations.

Total score: 0.505 Inference: inferred Novelty: 0.7 Feasibility: 0.6 Obvious-extension penalty: 0.2

Why retained: Retained as the next strongest distinct hypothesis because no clear incremental candidate was available.

Motivation: P4's generalization bound relies on contractivity, but P1's theory applies to a broader class of regular operators that may not be contractive. If the operator is not contractive, the fixed point may not be unique or the iteration may diverge. However, P1 shows that iterations progressively express more complex mappings, suggesting that the effective Lipschitz constant of the learned mapping may increase with iterations. A generalization bound that accounts for this could be derived by considering the Lipschitz constant of the fixed-point map as a function of iteration count (G2).

Novelty position: This would provide the first generalization guarantee for non-contractive implicit models, which are more common in practice, and link generalization to test-time compute.

How to test it: Train several implicit models with varying degrees of contractivity (e.g., spectral radius of Jacobian <1 vs >1). For each, estimate the Lipschitz constant of the fixed-point map after different numbers of test-time iterations. Compute the empirical generalization gap and compare to the theoretical bound derived from the Lipschitz constant.

Premise evidence: Evidence 6 Evidence 27

Supporting papers: P1: EXPRESSIVE POWER OF IMPLICIT MODELS: P4: A Generalization Bound for a Family of Implicit Networks

Risks:

  • The Lipschitz constant may be too large to yield a non-vacuous bound.
  • The analysis may require additional assumptions on the operator (e.g., differentiability) that are not always satisfied.

6. Hypothesis Quality Audit

This section audits the generated idea itself, not just the references attached to it. The hypothesis is decomposed into components such as idea core, motivation, novelty position, mechanism, feasibility, experiment, and risks. Each component receives a small relevance-ranked evidence bundle when directly relevant evidence exists; otherwise it is labeled as model-inferred or needing review. This avoids padding the hypothesis with weak citations while still showing which parts are grounded in evidence IDs.

Source-stated + GREENThe component restates source text with provenance; it is evidence, not novelty.
Evidence-inspired + YELLOWThe component is motivated by evidence but goes beyond what papers directly prove.
Model-inferred + YELLOWThe component is a generated bridge or mechanism; it needs human review and experiments.
Unsupported / REDThe component lacks evidence, contradicts evidence, or makes an unsupported strong claim.

Summary: Hypothesis quality audit separates what the literature directly says from what the model inferred as a new idea. Generated idea, novelty, and experiment components should usually be YELLOW unless directly restating an established finding.

Components: 16; by status: {'needs_human_review': 9, 'evidence_inspired': 6, 'model_inferred': 1}; by color: {'yellow': 16}; precision evidence per component: None

Evidence Crosswalk

This quick table expands the numbered evidence used by the hypothesis-quality rows below. Direct source-stated facts are shown here as evidence snippets rather than promoted into a separate premise table, because the generated hypothesis itself is still an inference unless its own claim-support row is GREEN. Reviewers can inspect the text, source paper, location, and per-use support relation without chasing raw hash IDs through the appendix. If one evidence item is used for multiple claims or components, each use is listed separately.

The theory is validated across four domains: imaging, scientific computing, operations research, and LLM reasoning, demonstrating that as test-time iterations increase, the complexity of the learned mapping rises, while the solution quality simultaneously improves and stabilizes.
Evidence 12literature map T1, literature map G4EXPRESSIVE POWER OF IMPLICIT MODELS:pdf:introduction:p2
literature map T1: context
literature map G4: context
Unlike studies focusing on infinite-width limits and kernel connections (Gao et al., 2022; Feng & Kolter, 2023; Ling et al., 2024), our work fills this gap from a nonparametric, function-space perspective, establishing that an implicit model’s expressive power scales with test-time compute. We identify locally Lipschitz mappings as a natural target class and prove: every such map F can be expressed as the fixed point of a “regular” (simple and wellbehaved) G, and conversely, every such fixed-point map is locally Lipschitz. Our theory, combined with iterative solvers’ dynamics, yields a new viewpoint on implicit models: the expressive power is progressively unlocked by iterations.
Evidence 15literature map T1Expressive Power of Implicit Models: Rich Equilibria and Test-Time Scalingpdf:abstract:p1
literature map T1: context
While it is empirically known that these compact models can often match or even exceed the accuracy of larger explicit networks by allocating more test-time compute, the underlying mechanism remains poorly understood. We provide a strict mathematical characterization, showing that a simple and regular implicit operator can, through iteration, progressively express more complex mappings. We prove that for a broad class of implicit models, this process lets the model’s expressive power scale with test-time compute, ultimately matching a much richer function class.
Evidence 16literature map T1Expressive Power of Implicit Models: Rich Equilibria and Test-Time Scalingpdf:abstract:p1
literature map T1: context
We prove that for a broad class of implicit models, this process lets the model’s expressive power scale with test-time compute, ultimately matching a much richer function class. The theory is validated across four domains: image reconstruction, scientific computing, operations research, and LLM reasoning, demonstrating that as test-time iterations increase, the complexity of the learned mapping rises, while the solution quality simultaneously improves and stabilizes.
Evidence 19literature map T1Expressive Power of Implicit Models: Rich Equilibria and Test-Time Scalingpdf:discussion:p12
literature map T1: context
Consistent with our theory, this growth reflects the model’s emergent capacity to map proximal inputs to semantically distinct outputs. Even in the discrete domain of language reasoning, iterating a fixed operator allows the model to scale its expressive power, evolving from simple surface-level processing to complex, context-aware reasoning. Conclusions We establish a sharp expressivity boundary for regular implicit models: as iterations increase, their expressive power grows, and the resulting fixed points can represent exactly the class of locally Lipschitz maps.

literature map G1: context
We prove, in this setting, that the spectral behavior of these Implicit-CKs and NTKs depend on the DEQ activation function and initial weight variances, but only via a system of four nonlinear equations.

As a direct consequence of this theoretical result, we demonstrate that a shallow explicit network can be carefully designed to produce the same CK or NTK as a given DEQ.

Evidence 31H1 premise, literature map T4, literature map G3, literature map X3On the Neural Tangent Kernel of Equilibrium Modelspdf:abstract:p1
H1 premise: context
literature map T4: context
literature map G3: context
literature map X3: context
This work studies the neural tangent kernel (NTK) of the deep equilibrium (DEQ) model, a practical “infinite-depth” architecture which directly computes the infinite-depth limit of a weight-tied network via root-finding. Even though the NTK of a fully-connected neural network can be stochastic if its width and depth both tend to infinity simultaneously, we show that contrarily a DEQ model still enjoys a deterministic NTK despite its width and depth going to infinity at the same time under mild conditions.
Evidence 32literature map T4On the Neural Tangent Kernel of Equilibrium Modelspdf:abstract:p1
literature map T4: context
Even though the NTK of a fully-connected neural network can be stochastic if its width and depth both tend to infinity simultaneously, we show that contrarily a DEQ model still enjoys a deterministic NTK despite its width and depth going to infinity at the same time under mild conditions. Moreover, this deterministic NTK can be found efficiently via root-finding.

This work studies the neural tangent kernel (NTK) of the deep equilibrium (DEQ) model, a practical “infinite-depth” architecture which directly computes the infinite-depth limit of a weight-tied network via root-finding. Even though the NTK of a fully-connected neural network can be stochastic if its width and depth both tend to infinity simultaneously, we show that contrarily a DEQ model still enjoys a deterministic NTK despite its width and depth going to infinity at the same time under mild conditions.

Even though the NTK of a fully-connected neural network can be stochastic if its width and depth both tend to infinity simultaneously, we show that contrarily a DEQ model still enjoys a deterministic NTK despite its width and depth going to infinity at the same time under mild conditions. Moreover, this deterministic NTK can be found efficiently via root-finding.
Evidence 45CL1 support, CL2 support, CL3 supportOn the Neural Tangent Kernel of Equilibrium Modelspdf:introduction:p1

It is well-known that at certain random initialization, neural networks of various structures converge to Gaussian processes as their widths go to infinity (Neal, 1996; Lee et al., 2017; Yang, 2019; Matthews et al., 2018; Novak et al., 2018; Garriga-Alonso et al., 2018). Recent deep learning theory advances have also shown that in the infinite width limit, with proper initialization (the NTK initialization), training the network fθ with gradient descent is equivalent to solving kernel regression with respect to the neural tangent kernel (NTK) (Arora et al., 2019; Jacot et al., 2018; Yang, 2019; Huang et al., 2020). In this work, we prove that with proper initialization, the NTK-of-DEQ enjoys a limit exchanging property limd→∞limn→∞Θ(d) n (x, y) = limn→∞limd→∞Θ(d) n (x, y) with high probability, where Θ(d) n denotes the empirical NTK of a neural network with d layers and n neurons each layer. The NTK-of-DEQ converges to meaningful deterministic fixed points that depend on the input in a non-trivial way, thus avoiding the freeze vs. Furthermore, analogous to DEQ models, we can compute these kernels by solving fixed point equations, rather than iteratively applying the updates as for traditional NTK.
Evidence 46CL1 support, CL2 support, CL3 supportOn the Neural Tangent Kernel of Equilibrium Modelspdf:introduction:p1
CL1 support: indirect overclaim

CL3 support: indirect
Uncertain or partial support: Available evidence motivates this generated claim, but it cannot directly prove the full claim.
Recent deep learning theory advances have also shown that in the infinite width limit, with proper initialization (the NTK initialization), training the network fθ with gradient descent is equivalent to solving kernel regression with respect to the neural tangent kernel (NTK) (Arora et al., 2019; Jacot et al., 2018; Yang, 2019; Huang et al., 2020). In this work, we prove that with proper initialization, the NTK-of-DEQ enjoys a limit exchanging property limd→∞limn→∞Θ(d) n (x, y) = limn→∞limd→∞Θ(d) n (x, y) with high probability, where Θ(d) n denotes the empirical NTK of a neural network with d layers and n neurons each layer. The NTK-of-DEQ converges to meaningful deterministic fixed points that depend on the input in a non-trivial way, thus avoiding the freeze vs. Furthermore, analogous to DEQ models, we can compute these kernels by solving fixed point equations, rather than iteratively applying the updates as for traditional NTK. We evaluate our approach and demonstrate that it matches the performance of existing regularized NTK methods.

We first show that unlike the infinite depth limit of NTK to FCNN, the DEQ-of-NTK does not converge to a degenerate kernel. This non-trivial kernel can be computed efficiently using root-finding.

Meanwhile, the infinite depth limit of NTK does not always present favorable properties. It turns out that the vanilla FCNN does not have a meaningful convergence: either it gives a constant kernel or the Kronecker Delta kernel (Jacot et al., 2019).

We use n, d to denote width and depth respectively, and write G(d) n to stress G has depth d and width n, where G can represent either a kernel or a neural network. We write G(d) = limn→∞G(d) n , Gn = limd→∞G(d) n , and G = limn,d→∞G(d) n to denote limits are taken. 3 NTK-of-DEQ with Fully-connected Layers In this section, we show how to derive the NTK of the fully-connected DEQ.

CL3 support: indirect
Uncertain or partial support: Available evidence motivates this generated claim, but it cannot directly prove the full claim.
In the infinite-width limit, Feng & Kolter (2023) formally derive the Neural Tangent Kernel (NTK) for equilibrium models, characterizing their training dynamics in the linear regime. (2024) show that for high-dimensional Gaussian mixtures, deep equilibrium models can be functionally equivalent to shallow explicit networks. In contrast to these kernel-based or distribution-specific analyses, our work adopts a non-parametric function-space perspective; we demonstrate that for general locally Lipschitz targets, the expressive power of implicit models is not static but scales dynamically with test-time computation, a property distinct from the linear regimes often studied in kernel theory. Its role was limited to grammar fixes, wording/flow improvements, and rephrasing of text that we originally drafted. All model suggestions were reviewed, verified, and, when necessary, edited by the authors to ensure accuracy.
Evidence 51CL3 supportEXPRESSIVE POWER OF IMPLICIT MODELS:pdf:introduction:p2

Uncertain or partial support: Available evidence motivates this generated claim, but it cannot directly prove the full claim.
Unlike studies focusing on infinite-width limits and kernel connections (Gao et al., 2022; Feng & Kolter, 2023; Ling et al., 2024), our work fills this gap from a nonparametric, function-space perspective, establishing that an implicit model’s expressive power scales with test-time compute. We identify locally Lipschitz mappings as a natural target class and prove: every such map F can be expressed as the fixed point of a “regular” (simple and wellbehaved) G, and conversely, every such fixed-point map is locally Lipschitz. Our theory, combined with iterative solvers’ dynamics, yields a new viewpoint on implicit models: the expressive power is progressively unlocked by iterations.
Evidence 52CL3 supportEXPRESSIVE POWER OF IMPLICIT MODELS:pdf:approach:p49
CL3 support: indirect
Uncertain or partial support: Available evidence motivates this generated claim, but it cannot directly prove the full claim.
Recent research has sought to bridge the gap between implicit models, explicit deep networks, and kernel methods. (2022) extend the over-parameterization theory of explicit networks to implicit models, establishing well-posedness and convergence even in finite-width regimes where standard infinite-depth results do not directly apply. In the infinite-width limit, Feng & Kolter (2023) formally derive the Neural Tangent Kernel (NTK) for equilibrium models, characterizing their training dynamics in the linear regime.

We prove that for a broad class of implicit models, this process allows the model’s expressive power to grow with test-time compute, ultimately matching a much richer function class. The theory is validated across four domains: imaging, scientific computing, operations research, and LLM reasoning, demonstrating that as test-time iterations increase, the complexity of the learned mapping rises, while the solution quality simultaneously improves and stabilizes.

While it is empirically known that these compact models can often match or even exceed the accuracy of larger explicit networks by allocating more test-time compute, the underlying reasons are not yet well understood. We provide a strict mathematical characterization, showing that a simple and regular implicit operator can, through iteration, progressively express more complex mappings. We prove that for a broad class of implicit models, this process allows the model’s expressive power to grow with test-time compute, ultimately matching a much richer function class.

We use n, d to denote width and depth respectively, and write G(d) n to stress G has depth d and width n, where G can represent either a kernel or a neural network. We write G(d) = limn→∞G(d) n , Gn = limd→∞G(d) n , and G = limn,d→∞G(d) n to denote limits are taken. 3 NTK-of-DEQ with Fully-connected Layers In this section, we show how to derive the NTK of the fully-connected DEQ. Define the depth-d approximation to a DEQ as the following: f (h) n (x) = r σ2 W n W (h)g(h−1)(x) + r σ2 U n U (h)x + r σ2 b n b(h), g(h) n (x) = σ(f (h)(x)), f (d+1) n (x) = σv · vT g(d)(x), where h ∈[d], W (h) ∈Rn×n, U (h) ∈Rn×m, v ∈Rn are the internal weights and b(h) ∈Rn are the bias terms. The forward pass is solved using root-finding or fixed point iteration, and the backward gradient is calculated using implicit function theorem instead of backpropogation.
Evidence 57CL3 supportOn the Neural Tangent Kernel of Equilibrium Modelspdf:background:p3
CL3 support: indirect
Uncertain or partial support: Available evidence motivates this generated claim, but it cannot directly prove the full claim.
We first show that unlike the infinite depth limit of NTK to FCNN, the DEQ-of-NTK does not converge to a degenerate kernel. This non-trivial kernel can be computed efficiently using root-finding. Although the proofs here involved infinite limits, we also show numerically that reasonably large networks converge to roughly the same quantities as predicted by theory, and we show the NTK-of-DEQ matches the performances of other NTKs on real-world datasets. 2.1 Notation We write capital letter W to represent matrices or tensors, which should be clear from the context, and use [W]i to represent the element of W indexed by i. We use n, d to denote width and depth respectively, and write G(d) n to stress G has depth d and width n, where G can represent either a kernel or a neural network.

H1idea_coreThe neural tangent kernel (NTK) of an implicit model changes with the number of test-time fixed-point iterations, contradicting the infinite-width NTK theory that predicts a fixed kernel.needs_human_reviewEvidence 6 Evidence 31 {'input_pdf': 1, 'full_text_pdf': 1}YELLOWAt least one claim-support row is RED, so the generated idea should be manually reviewed before use.The core idea is a generated research proposal. Existing evidence can motivate it, but it should not be read as already proven.
H1motivationP1 shows that the function class expressed by implicit models grows with iterations, while P5 claims the NTK is deterministic and fixed regardless of depth. If the NTK remains fixed, the increased expressivity must come from non-kernel aspects. However, it is possible that the NTK itself changes with iterations when the model is not in the infinite-width limit, or that the fixed-point iteration induces a different kernel. Testing this would bridge the gap between the two perspectives (G1, G3).evidence_inspiredEvidence 6 Evidence 31 {'input_pdf': 1, 'full_text_pdf': 1}YELLOWThis component is grounded in evidence IDs, but it remains a generated research inference rather than a directly proven conclusion.The motivation is checked for whether it is grounded in retrieved evidence rather than only sounding plausible.
H1noveltyThis hypothesis challenges the prevailing NTK theory for implicit models by suggesting that the kernel is not static but evolves with test-time compute, which would reconcile the expressivity scaling with kernel-based analyses.model_inferredEvidence 6 Evidence 31 {'input_pdf': 1, 'full_text_pdf': 1}YELLOWNo close prior work was found by available tools, but this does not prove the idea is new.Novelty claims are never GREEN merely because current tools did not find prior work.

H1feasibilityThe NTK can be computed empirically for finite-width DEQs at different iteration counts using standard autodiff tools. The experiment is straightforward: train a DEQ, then compute its empirical NTK after different numbers of fixed-point iterations at test time.evidence_inspiredEvidence 6 Evidence 31 {'input_pdf': 1, 'full_text_pdf': 1}YELLOWThis component is grounded in evidence IDs, but it remains a generated research inference rather than a directly proven conclusion.Feasibility arguments are useful planning judgments, not citation-verified conclusions.
H1experimentTrain a DEQ on a regression task. For a fixed set of test inputs, compute the empirical NTK matrix after k iterations (k=1,10,50,100) using the Jacobian of the network output with respect to parameters. Compare the NTK matrices across k; if they differ significantly, the hypothesis is supported.evidence_inspiredEvidence 6 Evidence 31 {'input_pdf': 1, 'full_text_pdf': 1}YELLOWThis component is grounded in evidence IDs, but it remains a generated research inference rather than a directly proven conclusion.The experiment is a falsification plan for the idea; it is not itself evidence that the idea is true.

H2idea_coreGeneralization bounds for implicit models that require contractivity can be extended to non-contractive regular operators by incorporating a Lipschitz constant that grows with the number of test-time iterations.needs_human_reviewEvidence 6 Evidence 27 {'input_pdf': 1, 'retrieved_abstract': 1}YELLOWAt least one claim-support row is RED, so the generated idea should be manually reviewed before use.The core idea is a generated research proposal. Existing evidence can motivate it, but it should not be read as already proven.
H2motivationP4's generalization bound relies on contractivity, but P1's theory applies to a broader class of regular operators that may not be contractive. If the operator is not contractive, the fixed point may not be unique or the iteration may diverge. However, P1 shows that iterations progressively express more complex mappings, suggesting that the effective Lipschitz constant of the learned mapping may increase with iterations. A generalization bound that accounts for this could be derived by considering the Lipschitz constant of the fixed-point map as a function of iteration count (G2).evidence_inspiredEvidence 6 Evidence 27 {'input_pdf': 1, 'retrieved_abstract': 1}YELLOWThis component is grounded in evidence IDs, but it remains a generated research inference rather than a directly proven conclusion.The motivation is checked for whether it is grounded in retrieved evidence rather than only sounding plausible.
H2noveltyThis would provide the first generalization guarantee for non-contractive implicit models, which are more common in practice, and link generalization to test-time compute.needs_human_reviewEvidence 6 Evidence 27 {'input_pdf': 1, 'retrieved_abstract': 1}YELLOWNovelty cannot be confirmed from available searches; absence of close work is not proof of novelty.Novelty claims are never GREEN merely because current tools did not find prior work.

H2feasibilityThe Lipschitz constant of the fixed-point map can be estimated using tools like AutoLip (P6). One can then compute a Rademacher complexity bound that depends on this Lipschitz constant and the number of iterations.evidence_inspiredEvidence 6 Evidence 27 {'input_pdf': 1, 'retrieved_abstract': 1}YELLOWThis component is grounded in evidence IDs, but it remains a generated research inference rather than a directly proven conclusion.Feasibility arguments are useful planning judgments, not citation-verified conclusions.
H2experimentTrain several implicit models with varying degrees of contractivity (e.g., spectral radius of Jacobian <1 vs >1). For each, estimate the Lipschitz constant of the fixed-point map after different numbers of test-time iterations. Compute the empirical generalization gap and compare to the theoretical bound derived from the Lipschitz constant.evidence_inspiredEvidence 6 Evidence 27 {'input_pdf': 1, 'retrieved_abstract': 1}YELLOWThis component is grounded in evidence IDs, but it remains a generated research inference rather than a directly proven conclusion.The experiment is a falsification plan for the idea; it is not itself evidence that the idea is true.

7. Hypothesis Novelty Search

This section searches for close prior work after the selected hypothesis is generated. After the primary hypothesis is selected, the system creates novelty-search queries from the hypothesis text, method terms, target problem, and supporting-paper neighborhood, then searches external literature again. The result is a risk assessment: exact/close matches suggest the idea may already exist, near matches suggest the idea needs sharper differentiation, related works provide background, and missing results only mean no close prior work was found by available tools. This section never proves novelty conclusively.

High riskExact or close prior work found; the idea likely needs substantial differentiation.
Medium riskNear work shares method, target problem, or framing; novelty may survive only with a sharper mechanism.
Low riskOnly background-related work found; still not proof of novelty.
Unknown riskSearch failed or evidence is too sparse; do not claim novelty.

Novelty risk: LOW

Conclusion: No close prior work was found by available tools, but related background work exists.

Reason: 6 related work(s) were found; absence of a close match is not proof of novelty.

8. Cited Reference Integrity Audit

This section audits only the references actually cited by the selected hypothesis atomic claims. It answers a bibliographic question: does the cited reference appear to exist, and do title, authors, year, DOI, venue, or source URL point to the same paper? It deliberately does not judge whether the reference supports the scientific claim; that is handled in the next section. Field status values separate matched/present metadata from missing or uncertain metadata. Missing DOI/year usually means the field was unavailable or not comparable, not automatically that the reference is wrong.

Score is bibliographic identity confidence, not a metadata-completeness percentage. A searched bibliographic record or local input PDF can score highly even when DOI or year is missing, but non-seed references with both DOI and year missing are downgraded to YELLOW.

GREEN integrityReference identity is verified enough for audit use; claim support is still judged separately.
YELLOW integrityReference probably exists but metadata is incomplete or uncertain; support cannot be upgraded to strong confidence.
RED integrityReference is not found or metadata conflicts strongly; related claim-support rows should be RED.
Missing DOI/yearUsually source coverage limitation, not a contradiction, unless other metadata also conflicts.
Reference IDInput / Matched ReferenceStatusScoreTitle statusAuthors statusYear statusDOI statusVenue statusColorReason

On the Neural Tangent Kernel of Equilibrium Models		verified0.9matchpresentpresentmissingpresentGREENBibliographic identity is verified by available metadata.

EXPRESSIVE POWER OF IMPLICIT MODELS:		verified1.0matchpresentmissingmissingpresentGREENLocal input PDF seed is treated as verified; missing DOI/year fields mean unavailable metadata, not a detected conflict.

9. Claim Support Audit

This section audits whether each cited reference supports the specific atomic claim attached to it. Rows in this table are claim-reference pairs, so the same claim ID may appear more than once if multiple references are checked separately. This is stricter than bibliographic integrity: a real paper can be GREEN in the previous section but YELLOW or RED here if it only provides background, supports a weaker claim, discusses a different topic, or lacks usable evidence snippets.

Support type rule: direct means verified reference + explicit evidence for the specific claim; indirect means topic-relevant evidence that motivates but does not prove the claim; background means weak/abstract-only context; absent means no usable evidence or no valid reference.

Error category rule: no_specific_error_detected means this row was not classified as unsupported claim, overclaim, mis-citation, or contradiction. It does not mean the claim is proven; use Color, Support, Support Type, Evidence IDs, and Reason for the final interpretation.

GREEN + directVerified reference and explicit evidence directly support this exact claim.
YELLOW + indirect/backgroundEvidence motivates or contextualizes the claim but does not prove it.
RED + unsupported/absentNo usable evidence, invalid reference, contradiction, or clear unsupported claim.
no_specific_error_detectedNo four-category error was detected; this can still be YELLOW if support is indirect.
Unsupported Claim0
Overclaim1
Mis-citation0
Contradiction0

CS1
Claim: CL1
REF-4fd123bcd3		P1 shows that the function class expressed by implicit models grows with iterations, while P5 claims the NTK is deterministic and fixed regardless of depth. If the NTK remains fixed, the increased expressivity must come from non-kernel aspects. However, it is possible that the NTK itself changes with iterations when the model is not in the infinite-width limit, or that the fixed-point iteration induces a different kernel. Testing this would bridge the gap between the two perspectives (G1, G3).partially_supportedindirectunsupportedoverclaimEvidence 43 Evidence 44 Evidence 45 Evidence 46 Evidence 47 Evidence 48 REDAudit category Overclaim: Unsupported, contradicted, or missing: Available evidence motivates this generated claim, but it cannot directly prove the full claim.

10. Evidence Cards

Evidence Cards expand evidence IDs into the human-readable form used throughout the report. Each card shows the source paper, URL or path, location, snippet text, and every claim or map item that reused the evidence. The same snippet can support multiple claims in different ways, so each use has its own support type, error category, and reason. Internal hash IDs are kept only in Appendix B for reproducibility; the numbered Evidence labels are intended for review.

One evidence, one useSimple case: one snippet is used for one claim or map item.
One evidence, multiple usesEach use has its own support type and reason; do not infer one global label for the snippet.
Abstract locationUseful but shallow; direct support from abstract alone should be interpreted cautiously.
PDF/full-text locationUsually stronger provenance because the snippet comes from a section or page-level source.

Evidence 12

Source paper: EXPRESSIVE POWER OF IMPLICIT MODELS:

Source URL / path: D:\SciHypoCheck\reports\openclaw_inputs\openclaw_input_20260608_072707.pdf

Location: pdf:introduction:p2; Used by: literature map T1, literature map G4

literature map T1contextno_specific_error_detected
literature map G4contextno_specific_error_detected
Unlike studies focusing on infinite-width limits and kernel connections (Gao et al., 2022; Feng & Kolter, 2023; Ling et al., 2024), our work fills this gap from a nonparametric, function-space perspective, establishing that an implicit model’s expressive power scales with test-time compute. We identify locally Lipschitz mappings as a natural target class and prove: every such map F can be expressed as the fixed point of a “regular” (simple and wellbehaved) G, and conversely, every such fixed-point map is locally Lipschitz. Our theory, combined with iterative solvers’ dynamics, yields a new viewpoint on implicit models: the expressive power is progressively unlocked by iterations.

Evidence 15

Source paper: Expressive Power of Implicit Models: Rich Equilibria and Test-Time Scaling

Source URL / path: https://arxiv.org/pdf/2510.03638

Location: pdf:abstract:p1; Used by: literature map T1

Used bySupport typeError categoryReason
literature map T1contextno_specific_error_detected
While it is empirically known that these compact models can often match or even exceed the accuracy of larger explicit networks by allocating more test-time compute, the underlying mechanism remains poorly understood. We provide a strict mathematical characterization, showing that a simple and regular implicit operator can, through iteration, progressively express more complex mappings. We prove that for a broad class of implicit models, this process lets the model’s expressive power scale with test-time compute, ultimately matching a much richer function class.

Evidence 16

Source paper: Expressive Power of Implicit Models: Rich Equilibria and Test-Time Scaling

Source URL / path: https://arxiv.org/pdf/2510.03638

Location: pdf:abstract:p1; Used by: literature map T1

Used bySupport typeError categoryReason
literature map T1contextno_specific_error_detected
We prove that for a broad class of implicit models, this process lets the model’s expressive power scale with test-time compute, ultimately matching a much richer function class. The theory is validated across four domains: image reconstruction, scientific computing, operations research, and LLM reasoning, demonstrating that as test-time iterations increase, the complexity of the learned mapping rises, while the solution quality simultaneously improves and stabilizes.

Evidence 19

Source paper: Expressive Power of Implicit Models: Rich Equilibria and Test-Time Scaling

Source URL / path: https://arxiv.org/pdf/2510.03638

Location: pdf:discussion:p12; Used by: literature map T1

Used bySupport typeError categoryReason
literature map T1contextno_specific_error_detected
Consistent with our theory, this growth reflects the model’s emergent capacity to map proximal inputs to semantically distinct outputs. Even in the discrete domain of language reasoning, iterating a fixed operator allows the model to scale its expressive power, evolving from simple surface-level processing to complex, context-aware reasoning. Conclusions We establish a sharp expressivity boundary for regular implicit models: as iterations increase, their expressive power grows, and the resulting fixed points can represent exactly the class of locally Lipschitz maps.

Evidence 31

Source paper: On the Neural Tangent Kernel of Equilibrium Models

Source URL / path: https://arxiv.org/pdf/2310.14062.pdf

literature map G3contextno_specific_error_detected
literature map X3contextno_specific_error_detected
This work studies the neural tangent kernel (NTK) of the deep equilibrium (DEQ) model, a practical “infinite-depth” architecture which directly computes the infinite-depth limit of a weight-tied network via root-finding. Even though the NTK of a fully-connected neural network can be stochastic if its width and depth both tend to infinity simultaneously, we show that contrarily a DEQ model still enjoys a deterministic NTK despite its width and depth going to infinity at the same time under mild conditions.

Evidence 32

Source paper: On the Neural Tangent Kernel of Equilibrium Models

Source URL / path: https://arxiv.org/pdf/2310.14062.pdf

Location: pdf:abstract:p1; Used by: literature map T4

Used bySupport typeError categoryReason
literature map T4contextno_specific_error_detected
Even though the NTK of a fully-connected neural network can be stochastic if its width and depth both tend to infinity simultaneously, we show that contrarily a DEQ model still enjoys a deterministic NTK despite its width and depth going to infinity at the same time under mild conditions. Moreover, this deterministic NTK can be found efficiently via root-finding.

Evidence 45 - indirect

Error category: overclaim

Source paper: On the Neural Tangent Kernel of Equilibrium Models

Source URL / path: https://arxiv.org/pdf/2310.14062.pdf

Location: pdf:introduction:p1; Used by: CL1 support, CL2 support, CL3 support

Used bySupport typeError categoryReason
CL1 supportindirectoverclaimAudit category Overclaim: Unsupported, contradicted, or missing: Available evidence motivates this generated claim, but it cannot directly prove the full claim.
CL2 supportindirectno_specific_error_detectedUncertain or partial support: Available evidence motivates this generated claim, but it cannot directly prove the full claim.
CL3 supportindirectno_specific_error_detectedUncertain or partial support: Available evidence motivates this generated claim, but it cannot directly prove the full claim.
It is well-known that at certain random initialization, neural networks of various structures converge to Gaussian processes as their widths go to infinity (Neal, 1996; Lee et al., 2017; Yang, 2019; Matthews et al., 2018; Novak et al., 2018; Garriga-Alonso et al., 2018). Recent deep learning theory advances have also shown that in the infinite width limit, with proper initialization (the NTK initialization), training the network fθ with gradient descent is equivalent to solving kernel regression with respect to the neural tangent kernel (NTK) (Arora et al., 2019; Jacot et al., 2018; Yang, 2019; Huang et al., 2020). In this work, we prove that with proper initialization, the NTK-of-DEQ enjoys a limit exchanging property limd→∞limn→∞Θ(d) n (x, y) = limn→∞limd→∞Θ(d) n (x, y) with high probability, where Θ(d) n denotes the empirical NTK of a neural network with d layers and n neurons each layer. The NTK-of-DEQ converges to meaningful deterministic fixed points that depend on the input in a non-trivial way, thus avoiding the freeze vs. Furthermore, analogous to DEQ models, we can compute these kernels by solving fixed point equations, rather than iteratively applying the updates as for traditional NTK.

Why: Audit category Overclaim: Unsupported, contradicted, or missing: Available evidence motivates this generated claim, but it cannot directly prove the full claim.

Location: pdf:introduction:p1; Used by: CL1 support, CL2 support, CL3 support

CL1 supportindirectoverclaimAudit category Overclaim: Unsupported, contradicted, or missing: Available evidence motivates this generated claim, but it cannot directly prove the full claim.
CL2 supportindirectno_specific_error_detectedUncertain or partial support: Available evidence motivates this generated claim, but it cannot directly prove the full claim.
CL3 supportindirectno_specific_error_detectedUncertain or partial support: Available evidence motivates this generated claim, but it cannot directly prove the full claim.
Recent deep learning theory advances have also shown that in the infinite width limit, with proper initialization (the NTK initialization), training the network fθ with gradient descent is equivalent to solving kernel regression with respect to the neural tangent kernel (NTK) (Arora et al., 2019; Jacot et al., 2018; Yang, 2019; Huang et al., 2020). In this work, we prove that with proper initialization, the NTK-of-DEQ enjoys a limit exchanging property limd→∞limn→∞Θ(d) n (x, y) = limn→∞limd→∞Θ(d) n (x, y) with high probability, where Θ(d) n denotes the empirical NTK of a neural network with d layers and n neurons each layer. The NTK-of-DEQ converges to meaningful deterministic fixed points that depend on the input in a non-trivial way, thus avoiding the freeze vs. Furthermore, analogous to DEQ models, we can compute these kernels by solving fixed point equations, rather than iteratively applying the updates as for traditional NTK. We evaluate our approach and demonstrate that it matches the performance of existing regularized NTK methods.

Why: Audit category Overclaim: Unsupported, contradicted, or missing: Available evidence motivates this generated claim, but it cannot directly prove the full claim.

We use n, d to denote width and depth respectively, and write G(d) n to stress G has depth d and width n, where G can represent either a kernel or a neural network. We write G(d) = limn→∞G(d) n , Gn = limd→∞G(d) n , and G = limn,d→∞G(d) n to denote limits are taken. 3 NTK-of-DEQ with Fully-connected Layers In this section, we show how to derive the NTK of the fully-connected DEQ.

Why: Uncertain or partial support: Available evidence motivates this generated claim, but it cannot directly prove the full claim.

Evidence 50 - indirect

Source paper: EXPRESSIVE POWER OF IMPLICIT MODELS:

Source URL / path: D:\SciHypoCheck\reports\openclaw_inputs\openclaw_input_20260608_072707.pdf

Location: pdf:approach:p49; Used by: CL3 support

Used bySupport typeError categoryReason
CL3 supportindirectno_specific_error_detectedUncertain or partial support: Available evidence motivates this generated claim, but it cannot directly prove the full claim.
In the infinite-width limit, Feng & Kolter (2023) formally derive the Neural Tangent Kernel (NTK) for equilibrium models, characterizing their training dynamics in the linear regime. (2024) show that for high-dimensional Gaussian mixtures, deep equilibrium models can be functionally equivalent to shallow explicit networks. In contrast to these kernel-based or distribution-specific analyses, our work adopts a non-parametric function-space perspective; we demonstrate that for general locally Lipschitz targets, the expressive power of implicit models is not static but scales dynamically with test-time computation, a property distinct from the linear regimes often studied in kernel theory. Its role was limited to grammar fixes, wording/flow improvements, and rephrasing of text that we originally drafted. All model suggestions were reviewed, verified, and, when necessary, edited by the authors to ensure accuracy.

Why: Uncertain or partial support: Available evidence motivates this generated claim, but it cannot directly prove the full claim.

Evidence 51 - indirect

Source paper: EXPRESSIVE POWER OF IMPLICIT MODELS:

Source URL / path: D:\SciHypoCheck\reports\openclaw_inputs\openclaw_input_20260608_072707.pdf

Location: pdf:introduction:p2; Used by: CL3 support

Used bySupport typeError categoryReason
CL3 supportindirectno_specific_error_detectedUncertain or partial support: Available evidence motivates this generated claim, but it cannot directly prove the full claim.
Unlike studies focusing on infinite-width limits and kernel connections (Gao et al., 2022; Feng & Kolter, 2023; Ling et al., 2024), our work fills this gap from a nonparametric, function-space perspective, establishing that an implicit model’s expressive power scales with test-time compute. We identify locally Lipschitz mappings as a natural target class and prove: every such map F can be expressed as the fixed point of a “regular” (simple and wellbehaved) G, and conversely, every such fixed-point map is locally Lipschitz. Our theory, combined with iterative solvers’ dynamics, yields a new viewpoint on implicit models: the expressive power is progressively unlocked by iterations.

Why: Uncertain or partial support: Available evidence motivates this generated claim, but it cannot directly prove the full claim.

Evidence 52 - indirect

Source paper: EXPRESSIVE POWER OF IMPLICIT MODELS:

Source URL / path: D:\SciHypoCheck\reports\openclaw_inputs\openclaw_input_20260608_072707.pdf

Location: pdf:approach:p49; Used by: CL3 support

Used bySupport typeError categoryReason
CL3 supportindirectno_specific_error_detectedUncertain or partial support: Available evidence motivates this generated claim, but it cannot directly prove the full claim.
Recent research has sought to bridge the gap between implicit models, explicit deep networks, and kernel methods. (2022) extend the over-parameterization theory of explicit networks to implicit models, establishing well-posedness and convergence even in finite-width regimes where standard infinite-depth results do not directly apply. In the infinite-width limit, Feng & Kolter (2023) formally derive the Neural Tangent Kernel (NTK) for equilibrium models, characterizing their training dynamics in the linear regime.

Why: Uncertain or partial support: Available evidence motivates this generated claim, but it cannot directly prove the full claim.

We prove that for a broad class of implicit models, this process allows the model’s expressive power to grow with test-time compute, ultimately matching a much richer function class. The theory is validated across four domains: imaging, scientific computing, operations research, and LLM reasoning, demonstrating that as test-time iterations increase, the complexity of the learned mapping rises, while the solution quality simultaneously improves and stabilizes.

Why: Uncertain or partial support: Available evidence motivates this generated claim, but it cannot directly prove the full claim.

While it is empirically known that these compact models can often match or even exceed the accuracy of larger explicit networks by allocating more test-time compute, the underlying reasons are not yet well understood. We provide a strict mathematical characterization, showing that a simple and regular implicit operator can, through iteration, progressively express more complex mappings. We prove that for a broad class of implicit models, this process allows the model’s expressive power to grow with test-time compute, ultimately matching a much richer function class.

Why: Uncertain or partial support: Available evidence motivates this generated claim, but it cannot directly prove the full claim.

We use n, d to denote width and depth respectively, and write G(d) n to stress G has depth d and width n, where G can represent either a kernel or a neural network. We write G(d) = limn→∞G(d) n , Gn = limd→∞G(d) n , and G = limn,d→∞G(d) n to denote limits are taken. 3 NTK-of-DEQ with Fully-connected Layers In this section, we show how to derive the NTK of the fully-connected DEQ. Define the depth-d approximation to a DEQ as the following: f (h) n (x) = r σ2 W n W (h)g(h−1)(x) + r σ2 U n U (h)x + r σ2 b n b(h), g(h) n (x) = σ(f (h)(x)), f (d+1) n (x) = σv · vT g(d)(x), where h ∈[d], W (h) ∈Rn×n, U (h) ∈Rn×m, v ∈Rn are the internal weights and b(h) ∈Rn are the bias terms. The forward pass is solved using root-finding or fixed point iteration, and the backward gradient is calculated using implicit function theorem instead of backpropogation.

We first show that unlike the infinite depth limit of NTK to FCNN, the DEQ-of-NTK does not converge to a degenerate kernel. This non-trivial kernel can be computed efficiently using root-finding. Although the proofs here involved infinite limits, we also show numerically that reasonably large networks converge to roughly the same quantities as predicted by theory, and we show the NTK-of-DEQ matches the performances of other NTKs on real-world datasets. 2.1 Notation We write capital letter W to represent matrices or tensors, which should be clear from the context, and use [W]i to represent the element of W indexed by i. We use n, d to denote width and depth respectively, and write G(d) n to stress G has depth d and width n, where G can represent either a kernel or a neural network.

11. Controlled Citation Audit Cases

This section is a controlled capability demonstration, separate from the selected hypothesis. The system constructs one direct-support case, one uncertain or inferred case, and one deliberately wrong or nonexistent-reference case. These probes show whether the audit machinery can produce GREEN, YELLOW, and RED under known conditions. They should not be interpreted as extra scientific claims made by the hypothesis generator.

GREEN controlled caseKnown direct support case; should have evidence and pass as GREEN.
YELLOW controlled caseGenerated or inferred claim; should not be forced to GREEN.
RED controlled caseFake reference, contradiction, or unsupported absolute claim; should never become GREEN.
Failing controlled caseIndicates an audit rule regression rather than a scientific finding.

12. Citation Verification Table

Legacy combined view. This is the combined citation-verification view retained from earlier reports. It merges bibliographic existence, metadata score, support label, evidence IDs, color, and reason into one table. For serious review, prefer Section 8 for reference identity and Section 9 for claim support, because those two questions are conceptually different. Exists + unsupportedThe paper is real but does not support the claim; check Section 9.

Low score + REDReference identity itself is doubtful or missing.
Evidence IDs presentUse Evidence Cards to inspect the actual snippet before trusting the row.
ClaimReferenceExistsBibliographic ScoreSupportSupport TypeConclusion StatusError CategoryEvidence IDsColorReason
P1 shows that the function class expressed by implicit models grows with iterations, while P5 claims the NTK is deterministic and fixed regardless of depth. If the NTK remains fixed, the increased expressivity must come from non-kernel aspects. However, it is possible that the NTK itself changes with iterations when the model is not in the infinite-width limit, or that the fixed-point iteration induces a different kernel. Testing this would bridge the gap between the two perspectives (G1, G3).P5 On the Neural Tangent Kernel of Equilibrium ModelsTrue0.9partially_supportedindirectunsupportedoverclaimEvidence 6 Evidence 31 Evidence 43 Evidence 44 Evidence 45 Evidence 46 Evidence 47 Evidence 48 REDAudit category Overclaim: Unsupported, contradicted, or missing: Available evidence motivates this generated claim, but it cannot directly prove the full claim.

14. Memory Influence Summary

This section explains the role of persistent SQLite memory in the current run. Memory can store prior papers, references, hypotheses, evidence records, rejected evidence, and verification outcomes. Retrieval does not automatically become evidence: previous memory may influence query planning, flag duplicate-risk, or warn about previously rejected material, but direct verification is reused only when the current claim, reference, evidence, and rule version match. If memory was retrieved but did not materially affect the run, the report says so explicitly.

Retrieved + materially alteredMemory changed query planning, duplicate-risk assessment, or direct verification reuse.
Retrieved + not usedMemory was available but treated as context only; this is acceptable and should be reported honestly.
Duplicate risk highA similar previous hypothesis exists; novelty should be interpreted cautiously.
Negative memoryPreviously rejected snippets or noisy candidates are warning signals, never scientific counter-evidence by themselves.

Memory influenced query planning/context through same-scope paper hits or memory-derived queries.

Negative Memory

These are previous rejected evidence snippets or failed candidates. They are warning-only memories: useful for avoiding repeated retrieval mistakes, identifying noisy sources, or explaining why a candidate was ignored, but never evidence that a scientific claim is false. Scientific support and contradiction must still come from current, inspectable evidence snippets.

15. Agent Reflection

This section is the agent's pre-report self-critique. The reflection tool checks whether the pipeline satisfied required ordering, whether retrieval found enough connected papers, whether novelty search ran, whether evidence is mostly abstract-level or full-text, whether reference integrity and claim support are credible, and whether memory materially influenced the result. Reflection findings are not hidden; they are reported as limitations or recommended next actions.

High severityMajor trust issue; rerun or inspect before using the result.
Medium severityInterpret with caution; usually evidence depth, sparse retrieval, or weak hypothesis score.
Low severityInformational limitation; useful for review but not necessarily blocking.
Passed checksPipeline invariants that succeeded, such as search-before-hypothesis or controlled audit behavior.

Summary: Reflection found no major structural problems, but the report remains limited by public API recall and available evidence depth.

Overall confidence: 0.92

Targeted rerun recommended: False

Findings

CategorySeverityIssueEvidenceRecommendation

Passed Checks

  • Relatedness gate kept 11 paper(s) and rejected 57 paper(s).
  • Literature search happened before hypothesis generation.
  • Novelty search happened after hypothesis generation and before claim verification.
  • Selected hypothesis portfolio contains 2 candidate(s).
  • Novelty search ran 3 query/queries.
  • Novelty search risk is low; no confirmed novelty is implied.
  • Evidence table contains 42 record(s).
  • Reference integrity audit produced 2 row(s): {'green': 2}.
  • Claim support audit produced 4 row(s): {'red': 1, 'yellow': 3}.
  • Controlled audit cases demonstrate GREEN, YELLOW, and RED behavior.
  • Memory influence summary is present.
  • Reference cleaning summary recorded 88 clean references and 6 unresolved fragment(s).

Recommended Next Actions

  • Use the current report as a first-pass audit, then manually inspect the highest-impact evidence cards.

16. Warnings and Limitations

This section collects automatic warnings and limitations detected during the run. Warnings are not automatic failures, but they mark places where a human reviewer should be cautious: shallow evidence, few retrieved papers, all-YELLOW claim support, high novelty risk, weak memory influence, API fallback, noisy reference extraction, or generated claims that remain mostly inferred.

No warningsThe automated checks found no obvious limitation, but human review is still required.
Evidence warningsSupport may be shallow, abstract-only, or missing full-text provenance.
Retrieval warningsFew connected papers, API fallback, or noisy search means the map may be incomplete.
Hypothesis warningsThe final idea may be mostly inferred, low-scoring, or duplicate-risk.
  • Some hypothesis components are model-inferred without direct evidence IDs; review the Hypothesis Quality Audit before treating them as conclusions.
  • Generated hypothesis support is mainly indirect, background, or absent.

Real API mode depends on public endpoints, available abstracts/full text, and external service coverage. Absence of retrieved close prior work is not proof of novelty.

Appendix A. Search and Retrieval Details

This appendix expands the retrieval pipeline. It shows the planned pre-generation queries, optional semantic-neighborhood expansion, kept papers, rejected papers, and the relatedness/citation-chain audit used to justify filtering. Kept papers are allowed into literature mapping and hypothesis generation; rejected papers are preserved for transparency but should not be treated as supporting evidence.

Kept paperPassed relatedness/citation-chain gate and can enter mapping or hypothesis generation.
Rejected paperRecorded for transparency but excluded from support and hypothesis generation.
Expansion enabledGraph neighborhood was sparse, so the system searched semantically nearby papers.
Expansion disabledConnected pool was already sufficient or run mode did not require expansion.

Semantic Neighborhood Expansion

Enabled: False. Not needed: connected paper pool was already large enough, or run is offline.

Query generation: not_run

Policy: Semantic expansion was not needed for this run.

Connected papers before expansion: 11; trigger threshold: 8; candidates added: 0.

Appendix B. Evidence and Reference Audit

This appendix expands evidence provenance and reference parsing details. It shows how the input seed was identified, how raw references were cleaned, every evidence record with source and location, resolved input references, verification snippets, and rejected evidence. Use it when checking whether an evidence number in the main report really corresponds to readable text from a traceable source.

Seed identity reliableDetected from filename, metadata, URL, OpenReview ID, or title match rather than arbitrary references.
Cleaned referenceReference parser produced a usable bibliographic string.
Unresolved fragmentLikely header, page number, broken DOI suffix, or incomplete citation; should not be treated as verified.
Rejected evidenceSnippet was boilerplate, front matter, off-topic, or low-value text and was excluded from support.
