Evidence IDTypeSourcePaperLocationConfidenceText
1(E-104d26f8718c)input_pdfD:\SciHypoCheck\reports\openclaw_inputs\openclaw_input_20260608_072707.pdfEXPRESSIVE POWER OF IMPLICIT MODELS:abstract sentence 11.0Implicit models, an emerging model class, compute outputs by iterating a sin- gle parameter block to a fixed point.
2(E-6b38440bdb02)input_pdfD:\SciHypoCheck\reports\openclaw_inputs\openclaw_input_20260608_072707.pdfEXPRESSIVE POWER OF IMPLICIT MODELS:abstract sentence 21.0This architecture realizes an infinite-depth, weight-tied network that trains with constant memory, significantly reducing memory needs for the same level of performance compared to explicit models.
3(E-c14e24913c5c)input_pdfD:\SciHypoCheck\reports\openclaw_inputs\openclaw_input_20260608_072707.pdfEXPRESSIVE POWER OF IMPLICIT MODELS:abstract sentence 31.0While it is empirically known that these compact models can often match or even exceed the accuracy of larger explicit networks by allocating more test-time com- pute, the underlying reasons are not yet well understood.
4(E-a8456cb46351)input_pdfD:\SciHypoCheck\reports\openclaw_inputs\openclaw_input_20260608_072707.pdfEXPRESSIVE POWER OF IMPLICIT MODELS:abstract sentence 41.0We study this gap through a non-parametric analysis of expressive power.
5(E-0f6dd8e0ef31)input_pdfD:\SciHypoCheck\reports\openclaw_inputs\openclaw_input_20260608_072707.pdfEXPRESSIVE POWER OF IMPLICIT MODELS:abstract sentence 51.0We provide a strict mathematical characterization, showing that a simple and regular implicit operator can, through iteration, progressively express more complex map- pings.
6(E-ef2c01b7d35a)input_pdfD:\SciHypoCheck\reports\openclaw_inputs\openclaw_input_20260608_072707.pdfEXPRESSIVE POWER OF IMPLICIT MODELS:abstract sentence 61.0We prove that for a broad class of implicit models, this process allows the model’s expressive power to grow with test-time compute, ultimately matching a much richer function class.
7(E-a4d44403a92f)input_pdfD:\SciHypoCheck\reports\openclaw_inputs\openclaw_input_20260608_072707.pdfEXPRESSIVE POWER OF IMPLICIT MODELS:abstract sentence 71.0The theory is validated across four domains: imaging, scientific computing, operations research, and LLM reasoning, demonstrating that as test-time iterations increase, the complexity of the learned mapping rises, while the solution quality simultaneously improves and stabilizes.
8(E-53bf17e86efc)full_text_pdfinput_pdfEXPRESSIVE POWER OF IMPLICIT MODELS:pdf:abstract:p10.95While it is empirically known that these compact models can often match or even exceed the accuracy of larger explicit networks by allocating more test-time compute, the underlying reasons are not yet well understood. We provide a strict mathematical characterization, showing that a simple and regular implicit operator can, through iteration, progressively express more complex mappings. We prove that for a broad class of implicit models, this process allows the model’s expressive power to grow with test-time compute, ultimately matching a much richer function class.
9(E-2406f45fde75)full_text_pdfinput_pdfEXPRESSIVE POWER OF IMPLICIT MODELS:pdf:introduction:p20.95While universality has been touched upon in specific settings (Bai et al., 2019; Marwah et al., 2023) and separation results have demonstrated advantages over explicit models (Wu et al., 2024), a complete characterization of the representable function class of implicit models (and hence a direct answer to questions (Q1) and (Q2)) is still missing. Unlike studies focusing on infinite-width limits and kernel connections (Gao et al., 2022; Feng & Kolter, 2023; Ling et al., 2024), our work fills this gap from a nonparametric, function-space perspective, establishing that an implicit model’s expressive power scales with test-time compute. We identify locally Lipschitz mappings as a natural target class and prove: every such map F can be expressed as the fixed point of a “regular” (simple and wellbehaved) G, and conversely, every such fixed-point map is locally Lipschitz.
10(E-94adf4a12de7)full_text_pdfinput_pdfEXPRESSIVE POWER OF IMPLICIT MODELS:pdf:introduction:p20.95In particular, can a relatively simple implicit operator G, through iteration, represent a complex explicit map F? While universality has been touched upon in specific settings (Bai et al., 2019; Marwah et al., 2023) and separation results have demonstrated advantages over explicit models (Wu et al., 2024), a complete characterization of the representable function class of implicit models (and hence a direct answer to questions (Q1) and (Q2)) is still missing. Unlike studies focusing on infinite-width limits and kernel connections (Gao et al., 2022; Feng & Kolter, 2023; Ling et al., 2024), our work fills this gap from a nonparametric, function-space perspective, establishing that an implicit model’s expressive power scales with test-time compute.
11(E-e75831b345d0)full_text_pdfinput_pdfEXPRESSIVE POWER OF IMPLICIT MODELS:pdf:abstract:p10.95We prove that for a broad class of implicit models, this process allows the model’s expressive power to grow with test-time compute, ultimately matching a much richer function class. The theory is validated across four domains: imaging, scientific computing, operations research, and LLM reasoning, demonstrating that as test-time iterations increase, the complexity of the learned mapping rises, while the solution quality simultaneously improves and stabilizes.
12(E-f6d21ba2c553)full_text_pdfinput_pdfEXPRESSIVE POWER OF IMPLICIT MODELS:pdf:introduction:p20.95Unlike studies focusing on infinite-width limits and kernel connections (Gao et al., 2022; Feng & Kolter, 2023; Ling et al., 2024), our work fills this gap from a nonparametric, function-space perspective, establishing that an implicit model’s expressive power scales with test-time compute. We identify locally Lipschitz mappings as a natural target class and prove: every such map F can be expressed as the fixed point of a “regular” (simple and wellbehaved) G, and conversely, every such fixed-point map is locally Lipschitz. Our theory, combined with iterative solvers’ dynamics, yields a new viewpoint on implicit models: the expressive power is progressively unlocked by iterations.
13(E-f261d552c740)full_text_pdfinput_pdfEXPRESSIVE POWER OF IMPLICIT MODELS:pdf:introduction:p20.95Our theory, combined with iterative solvers’ dynamics, yields a new viewpoint on implicit models: the expressive power is progressively unlocked by iterations. We validate our theory with case studies in a wide range of applications (e.g., image reconstruction, scientific computing, operations research, and LLM reasoning). Note that, while explicit networks are capable of expressing locally Lipschitz target maps (Beneventano et al., 2021) by scaling up the model size, implicit models can scale expressivity with test-time compute (i.e., more iterations at inference), which scales up test-time runtime but not parameters. This prompts the natural follow-up: is there any nontrivial implicit representation that is able to indicate the expressive benefits of implicit models? Neural networks approximating 1/x on [−1, −δ) ∪(δ, 1] typically demands higher network complexity—i.e., increasing depth/width as δ →0 to capture the growing steepness near the singularity (Telgarsky, 2017).
14(E-e7a12e5282d2)full_text_pdfarxivExpressive Power of Implicit Models: Rich Equilibria and Test-Time Scalingpdf:results:p20.95While universality has been touched upon in specific settings [3, 73] and separation results have demonstrated advantages over explicit models [106], a complete characterization of the representable function class of implicit models (and hence a direct answer to questions (Q1) and (Q2)) is still missing. Unlike studies focusing on infinite-width limits and kernel connections [29,33,63], our work fills this gap from a nonparametric, function-space perspective, establishing that an implicit model’s expressive power scales with test-time compute.
15(E-9889a37ce32f)full_text_pdfarxivExpressive Power of Implicit Models: Rich Equilibria and Test-Time Scalingpdf:abstract:p10.95While it is empirically known that these compact models can often match or even exceed the accuracy of larger explicit networks by allocating more test-time compute, the underlying mechanism remains poorly understood. We provide a strict mathematical characterization, showing that a simple and regular implicit operator can, through iteration, progressively express more complex mappings. We prove that for a broad class of implicit models, this process lets the model’s expressive power scale with test-time compute, ultimately matching a much richer function class.
16(E-430566fe09ad)full_text_pdfarxivExpressive Power of Implicit Models: Rich Equilibria and Test-Time Scalingpdf:abstract:p10.95We prove that for a broad class of implicit models, this process lets the model’s expressive power scale with test-time compute, ultimately matching a much richer function class. The theory is validated across four domains: image reconstruction, scientific computing, operations research, and LLM reasoning, demonstrating that as test-time iterations increase, the complexity of the learned mapping rises, while the solution quality simultaneously improves and stabilizes.
17(E-063264a54711)full_text_pdfarxivExpressive Power of Implicit Models: Rich Equilibria and Test-Time Scalingpdf:appendix:p470.95While our work primarily establishes the expressive power of implicit models through the lens of fixed-point iterations, we situate our contributions within the broader landscape of implicit model theory in this section. For example, while [3] demonstrated that an operator g exists such that its fixed point reproduces any explicit network f, this existence result crucially does not guarantee that the fixed-point iteration (1) actually converges. Other works have established universality within restricted domains, such as steady-state PDEs [73], or proven separation results where implicit models outperform explicit counterparts in specific settings [106]. While insightful, none of these studies provide a complete characterization of the general function class representable by implicit models, nor do they directly address the fundamental questions (Q1) and (Q2) raised in our introduction.
18(E-774bba145295)full_text_pdfarxivExpressive Power of Implicit Models: Rich Equilibria and Test-Time Scalingpdf:appendix:p20.95Our theory, combined with the root-finding solvers’ dynamics, yields a new viewpoint on implicit models: the expressive power of a regular implicit operator is not static but progressively unlocked by iterations (i.e., scales with test-time compute), and finally matches a much richer function class. We validate our theory with case studies in a wide range of applications (e.g., image reconstruction, scientific computing, operations research, and LLM reasoning). For a representative problem in each domain, we demonstrating that as test-time iterations increase, the empirical complexity of the iterate yt(x) rises while solution quality improves and stabilizes.
19(E-7f2ca99a93d9)full_text_pdfarxivExpressive Power of Implicit Models: Rich Equilibria and Test-Time Scalingpdf:discussion:p120.95Consistent with our theory, this growth reflects the model’s emergent capacity to map proximal inputs to semantically distinct outputs. Even in the discrete domain of language reasoning, iterating a fixed operator allows the model to scale its expressive power, evolving from simple surface-level processing to complex, context-aware reasoning. Conclusions We establish a sharp expressivity boundary for regular implicit models: as iterations increase, their expressive power grows, and the resulting fixed points can represent exactly the class of locally Lipschitz maps.
20(E-4c9c96d76c49)retrieved_abstractopenreviewDeep Equilibrium Models are Almost Equivalent to Not-so-deep Explicit Models for High-dimensional Gaussian Mixturesabstract0.907We prove, in this setting, that the spectral behavior of these Implicit-CKs and NTKs depend on the DEQ activation function and initial weight variances, but only via a system of four nonlinear equations.
21(E-a6f997b10fbb)retrieved_abstractopenreviewDeep Equilibrium Models are Almost Equivalent to Not-so-deep Explicit Models for High-dimensional Gaussian Mixturesabstract0.904In this paper, leveraging recent advances in random matrix theory (RMT), we perform an in-depth analysis on the eigenspectra of the conjugate kernel (CK) and neural tangent kernel (NTK) matrices for implicit DEQs, when the input data are drawn from a high-dimensional Gaussian mixture.
22(E-075165918a83)retrieved_abstractopenreviewDeep Equilibrium Models are Almost Equivalent to Not-so-deep Explicit Models for High-dimensional Gaussian Mixturesabstract0.902Deep equilibrium models (DEQs), as a typical implicit neural network, have demonstrated remarkable success on various tasks.
23(E-93af6c015ed7)retrieved_abstractopenreviewDeep Equilibrium Models are Almost Equivalent to Not-so-deep Explicit Models for High-dimensional Gaussian Mixturesabstract0.9There is, however, a lack of theoretical understanding of the connections and differences between implicit DEQs and explicit neural network models.
24(E-527e23c5f37e)retrieved_abstractopenreviewDeep Equilibrium Models are Almost Equivalent to Not-so-deep Explicit Models for High-dimensional Gaussian Mixturesabstract0.896As a direct consequence of this theoretical result, we demonstrate that a shallow explicit network can be carefully designed to produce the same CK or NTK as a given DEQ.
25(E-6b9471f9dbd9)retrieved_abstractopenreviewDeep Equilibrium Models are Almost Equivalent to Not-so-deep Explicit Models for High-dimensional Gaussian Mixturesabstract0.888Despite derived here for Gaussian mixture data, empirical results show the proposed theory and design principle also apply to popular real-world datasets.
26(E-1d5e12111fbe)retrieved_abstractopenreviewA Generalization Bound for a Family of Implicit Networksabstract0.928Implicit networks are a class of neural networks whose outputs are defined by the fixed point of a parameterized operator.
27(E-fb166f6208c3)retrieved_abstractopenreviewA Generalization Bound for a Family of Implicit Networksabstract0.917In this work, we consider a large family of implicit networks defined parameterized contractive fixed point operators.
28(E-18a5a726f8a4)retrieved_abstractopenreviewA Generalization Bound for a Family of Implicit Networksabstract0.889We show a generalization bound for this class based on a covering number argument for the Rademacher complexity of these architectures.
29(E-d95249079c6e)retrieved_abstractopenreviewA Generalization Bound for a Family of Implicit Networksabstract0.851They have enjoyed success in many applications including natural language processing, image processing, and numerous other applications.
30(E-7a88263eecd8)retrieved_abstractopenreviewA Generalization Bound for a Family of Implicit Networksabstract0.836While they have found abundant empirical success, theoretical work on its generalization is still under-explored.
31(E-2a0765285b00)full_text_pdfarxivOn the Neural Tangent Kernel of Equilibrium Modelspdf:abstract:p10.95This work studies the neural tangent kernel (NTK) of the deep equilibrium (DEQ) model, a practical “infinite-depth” architecture which directly computes the infinite-depth limit of a weight-tied network via root-finding. Even though the NTK of a fully-connected neural network can be stochastic if its width and depth both tend to infinity simultaneously, we show that contrarily a DEQ model still enjoys a deterministic NTK despite its width and depth going to infinity at the same time under mild conditions.
32(E-69f5faf264a4)full_text_pdfarxivOn the Neural Tangent Kernel of Equilibrium Modelspdf:abstract:p10.95Even though the NTK of a fully-connected neural network can be stochastic if its width and depth both tend to infinity simultaneously, we show that contrarily a DEQ model still enjoys a deterministic NTK despite its width and depth going to infinity at the same time under mild conditions. Moreover, this deterministic NTK can be found efficiently via root-finding.
33(E-f13e7df96f77)full_text_pdfarxivOn the Neural Tangent Kernel of Equilibrium Modelspdf:introduction:p10.95Implicit models form a new class of machine learning models where instead of stacking explicit “layers”, they output z s.t g(x, z) = 0, where g can be either a fixed point equation (Bai et al., 2019), a differential equation (Chen et al., 2018b), or an optimization problem (Gould et al., 2019). This work focuses on deep equilibrium models, a class of models that effectively represent a “infinite-depth” weight-tied network with input injection.
34(E-cf4bb970acfa)full_text_pdfarxivOn the Neural Tangent Kernel of Equilibrium Modelspdf:background:p20.948A weight-tied FCNN with input injection (FCNN-IJ) makes the bias term related to the original input and ties the weight in each layer by taking the form z(t) := f(z(t−1), x) = σ(Wz(t−1) + Ux + b). (2019) proposed the DEQ model, which can be equivalent to running an infinite-depth FCNN-IJ, but updated in a more clever way. The backward iteration is done by computing df(z∗, x)/dz∗ directly through the implicit function theorem, thus avoiding storing the Jacobian for each layer. This method traces back to some of the original work in recurrent backpropagation (Almeida, 1990; Pineda, 1988), but with specific emphasis on: 1) computing the fixed point directly via root-finding rather than forward iteration; and 2) incorporating the elements from modern deep networks in the single “layer”, such as self-attention transformers (Bai et al., 2019), multi-scale convolutions (Bai et al., 2020), etc. DEQ models achieve nearly state-of-the-art performances on many large-scale tasks including the CityScape semantic segmentation and ImageNet classification, while only requiring constant memory.
35(E-d78f1d612011)full_text_pdfarxivOn the Neural Tangent Kernel of Equilibrium Modelspdf:introduction:p10.864Recent deep learning theory advances have also shown that in the infinite width limit, with proper initialization (the NTK initialization), training the network fθ with gradient descent is equivalent to solving kernel regression with respect to the neural tangent kernel (NTK) (Arora et al., 2019; Jacot et al., 2018; Yang, 2019; Huang et al., 2020). In this work, we prove that with proper initialization, the NTK-of-DEQ enjoys a limit exchanging property limd→∞limn→∞Θ(d) n (x, y) = limn→∞limd→∞Θ(d) n (x, y) with high probability, where Θ(d) n denotes the empirical NTK of a neural network with d layers and n neurons each layer. The NTK-of-DEQ converges to meaningful deterministic fixed points that depend on the input in a non-trivial way, thus avoiding the freeze vs. Furthermore, analogous to DEQ models, we can compute these kernels by solving fixed point equations, rather than iteratively applying the updates as for traditional NTK. We evaluate our approach and demonstrate that it matches the performance of existing regularized NTK methods.
36(E-79e7ae48d66b)full_text_pdfarxivOn the Neural Tangent Kernel of Equilibrium Modelspdf:introduction:p10.862It is well-known that at certain random initialization, neural networks of various structures converge to Gaussian processes as their widths go to infinity (Neal, 1996; Lee et al., 2017; Yang, 2019; Matthews et al., 2018; Novak et al., 2018; Garriga-Alonso et al., 2018). Recent deep learning theory advances have also shown that in the infinite width limit, with proper initialization (the NTK initialization), training the network fθ with gradient descent is equivalent to solving kernel regression with respect to the neural tangent kernel (NTK) (Arora et al., 2019; Jacot et al., 2018; Yang, 2019; Huang et al., 2020). In this work, we prove that with proper initialization, the NTK-of-DEQ enjoys a limit exchanging property limd→∞limn→∞Θ(d) n (x, y) = limn→∞limd→∞Θ(d) n (x, y) with high probability, where Θ(d) n denotes the empirical NTK of a neural network with d layers and n neurons each layer. The NTK-of-DEQ converges to meaningful deterministic fixed points that depend on the input in a non-trivial way, thus avoiding the freeze vs. Furthermore, analogous to DEQ models, we can compute these kernels by solving fixed point equations, rather than iteratively applying the updates as for traditional NTK.
37(E-553039f5929d)full_text_pdfarxivLipschitz regularity of deep neural networks: analysis and efficient estimationpdf:abstract:p10.95Deep neural networks are notorious for being sensitive to small well-chosen perturbations, and estimating the regularity of such architectures is of utmost importance for safe and robust practical applications. In this paper, we investigate one of the key characteristics to assess the regularity of such methods: the Lipschitz constant of deep learning architectures.
38(E-d12aca6d14f1)full_text_pdfarxivLipschitz regularity of deep neural networks: analysis and efficient estimationpdf:method:p10.95previous estimation methods by providing AutoLip, the ﬁrst generic algorithm for upper bounding the Lipschitz constant of any automatically differentiable function. Second, for sequential neural networks, we propose an improved algorithm named SeqLip that takes advantage of the linear computation graph to split the computation per pair of consecutive layers.
39(E-ce2a82498b9c)full_text_pdfarxivLipschitz regularity of deep neural networks: analysis and efficient estimationpdf:method:p10.95Second, for sequential neural networks, we propose an improved algorithm named SeqLip that takes advantage of the linear computation graph to split the computation per pair of consecutive layers. Our experiments show that SeqLip can signiﬁcantly improve on the existing upper bounds. Finally, we provide an implementation of AutoLip in the PyTorch environment that may be used to better estimate the robustness of a given neural network to small perturbations or regularize it using more precise Lipschitz estimations.
40(E-c7f25a378e5d)retrieved_abstractarxivLipschitz regularity of deep neural networks: analysis and efficient estimationabstract0.91We provide a power method algorithm working with automatic differentiation, allowing efficient computations even on large convolutions.
41(E-cce73dcb4e3c)retrieved_abstractarxivLipschitz regularity of deep neural networks: analysis and efficient estimationabstract0.882First, we show that, even for two layer neural networks, the exact computation of this quantity is NP-hard and state-of-art methods may significantly overestimate it.
42(E-d0ed3db93a59)retrieved_abstractarxivLipschitz regularity of deep neural networks: analysis and efficient estimationabstract0.875Then, we both extend and improve previous estimation methods by providing AutoLip, the first generic algorithm for upper bounding the Lipschitz constant of any automatically differentiable function.

Evidence Snippets with Provenance

CL1 - RED

This work studies the neural tangent kernel (NTK) of the deep equilibrium (DEQ) model, a practical “infinite-depth” architecture which directly computes the infinite-depth limit of a weight-tied network via root-finding. Even though the NTK of a fully-connected neural network can be stochastic if its width and depth both tend to infinity simultaneously, we show that contrarily a DEQ model still enjoys a deterministic NTK despite its width and depth going to infinity at the same time under mild conditions.
Evidence ID: 43 (E-b83146bac2d3); Source: arxiv; Location: pdf:abstract:p1; Paper: On the Neural Tangent Kernel of Equilibrium Models; URL: https://arxiv.org/pdf/2310.14062.pdf; Confidence: 0.95
Even though the NTK of a fully-connected neural network can be stochastic if its width and depth both tend to infinity simultaneously, we show that contrarily a DEQ model still enjoys a deterministic NTK despite its width and depth going to infinity at the same time under mild conditions. Moreover, this deterministic NTK can be found efficiently via root-finding.
Evidence ID: 44 (E-cc8851397b05); Source: arxiv; Location: pdf:abstract:p1; Paper: On the Neural Tangent Kernel of Equilibrium Models; URL: https://arxiv.org/pdf/2310.14062.pdf; Confidence: 0.95
It is well-known that at certain random initialization, neural networks of various structures converge to Gaussian processes as their widths go to infinity (Neal, 1996; Lee et al., 2017; Yang, 2019; Matthews et al., 2018; Novak et al., 2018; Garriga-Alonso et al., 2018). Recent deep learning theory advances have also shown that in the infinite width limit, with proper initialization (the NTK initialization), training the network fθ with gradient descent is equivalent to solving kernel regression with respect to the neural tangent kernel (NTK) (Arora et al., 2019; Jacot et al., 2018; Yang, 2019; Huang et al., 2020). In this work, we prove that with proper initialization, the NTK-of-DEQ enjoys a limit exchanging property limd→∞limn→∞Θ(d) n (x, y) = limn→∞limd→∞Θ(d) n (x, y) with high probability, where Θ(d) n denotes the empirical NTK of a neural network with d layers and n neurons each layer. The NTK-of-DEQ converges to meaningful deterministic fixed points that depend on the input in a non-trivial way, thus avoiding the freeze vs. Furthermore, analogous to DEQ models, we can compute these kernels by solving fixed point equations, rather than iteratively applying the updates as for traditional NTK.
Evidence ID: 45 (E-7acfc0965a3b); Source: arxiv; Location: pdf:introduction:p1; Paper: On the Neural Tangent Kernel of Equilibrium Models; URL: https://arxiv.org/pdf/2310.14062.pdf; Confidence: 0.908
Recent deep learning theory advances have also shown that in the infinite width limit, with proper initialization (the NTK initialization), training the network fθ with gradient descent is equivalent to solving kernel regression with respect to the neural tangent kernel (NTK) (Arora et al., 2019; Jacot et al., 2018; Yang, 2019; Huang et al., 2020). In this work, we prove that with proper initialization, the NTK-of-DEQ enjoys a limit exchanging property limd→∞limn→∞Θ(d) n (x, y) = limn→∞limd→∞Θ(d) n (x, y) with high probability, where Θ(d) n denotes the empirical NTK of a neural network with d layers and n neurons each layer. The NTK-of-DEQ converges to meaningful deterministic fixed points that depend on the input in a non-trivial way, thus avoiding the freeze vs. Furthermore, analogous to DEQ models, we can compute these kernels by solving fixed point equations, rather than iteratively applying the updates as for traditional NTK. We evaluate our approach and demonstrate that it matches the performance of existing regularized NTK methods.
Evidence ID: 46 (E-d9a215290876); Source: arxiv; Location: pdf:introduction:p1; Paper: On the Neural Tangent Kernel of Equilibrium Models; URL: https://arxiv.org/pdf/2310.14062.pdf; Confidence: 0.904
We first show that unlike the infinite depth limit of NTK to FCNN, the DEQ-of-NTK does not converge to a degenerate kernel. This non-trivial kernel can be computed efficiently using root-finding.
Evidence ID: 47 (E-bbc114dceb22); Source: arxiv; Location: pdf:background:p3; Paper: On the Neural Tangent Kernel of Equilibrium Models; URL: https://arxiv.org/pdf/2310.14062.pdf; Confidence: 0.835
Meanwhile, the infinite depth limit of NTK does not always present favorable properties. It turns out that the vanilla FCNN does not have a meaningful convergence: either it gives a constant kernel or the Kronecker Delta kernel (Jacot et al., 2019).
Evidence ID: 48 (E-2e307f0dab6d); Source: arxiv; Location: pdf:background:p2; Paper: On the Neural Tangent Kernel of Equilibrium Models; URL: https://arxiv.org/pdf/2310.14062.pdf; Confidence: 0.805

The NTK-of-DEQ converges to meaningful deterministic fixed points that depend on the input in a non-trivial way, thus avoiding the freeze vs. Furthermore, analogous to DEQ models, we can compute these kernels by solving fixed point equations, rather than iteratively applying the updates as for traditional NTK. Recent deep learning theory advances have also shown that in the infinite width limit, with proper initialization (the NTK initialization), training the network fθ with gradient descent is equivalent to solving kernel regression with respect to the neural tangent kernel (NTK) (Arora et al., 2019; Jacot et al., 2018; Yang, 2019; Huang et al., 2020). Evidence ID: 47 (E-bbc114dceb22); Source: arxiv; Location: pdf:background:p3; Paper: On the Neural Tangent Kernel of Equilibrium Models; URL: https://arxiv.org/pdf/2310.14062.pdf; Confidence: 0.82
We use n, d to denote width and depth respectively, and write G(d) n to stress G has depth d and width n, where G can represent either a kernel or a neural network. We write G(d) = limn→∞G(d) n , Gn = limd→∞G(d) n , and G = limn,d→∞G(d) n to denote limits are taken. 3 NTK-of-DEQ with Fully-connected Layers In this section, we show how to derive the NTK of the fully-connected DEQ.
Evidence ID: 49 (E-76a2e04f58c8); Source: arxiv; Location: pdf:background:p3; Paper: On the Neural Tangent Kernel of Equilibrium Models; URL: https://arxiv.org/pdf/2310.14062.pdf; Confidence: 0.781

CL3 - YELLOW

In the infinite-width limit, Feng & Kolter (2023) formally derive the Neural Tangent Kernel (NTK) for equilibrium models, characterizing their training dynamics in the linear regime. (2024) show that for high-dimensional Gaussian mixtures, deep equilibrium models can be functionally equivalent to shallow explicit networks. In contrast to these kernel-based or distribution-specific analyses, our work adopts a non-parametric function-space perspective; we demonstrate that for general locally Lipschitz targets, the expressive power of implicit models is not static but scales dynamically with test-time computation, a property distinct from the linear regimes often studied in kernel theory. Its role was limited to grammar fixes, wording/flow improvements, and rephrasing of text that we originally drafted. All model suggestions were reviewed, verified, and, when necessary, edited by the authors to ensure accuracy.
Evidence ID: 50 (E-26a52c3ee347); Source: input_pdf; Location: pdf:approach:p49; Paper: EXPRESSIVE POWER OF IMPLICIT MODELS:; URL: D:\SciHypoCheck\reports\openclaw_inputs\openclaw_input_20260608_072707.pdf; Confidence: 0.95
Unlike studies focusing on infinite-width limits and kernel connections (Gao et al., 2022; Feng & Kolter, 2023; Ling et al., 2024), our work fills this gap from a nonparametric, function-space perspective, establishing that an implicit model’s expressive power scales with test-time compute. We identify locally Lipschitz mappings as a natural target class and prove: every such map F can be expressed as the fixed point of a “regular” (simple and wellbehaved) G, and conversely, every such fixed-point map is locally Lipschitz. Our theory, combined with iterative solvers’ dynamics, yields a new viewpoint on implicit models: the expressive power is progressively unlocked by iterations.
Evidence ID: 51 (E-2d2782b2d5e5); Source: input_pdf; Location: pdf:introduction:p2; Paper: EXPRESSIVE POWER OF IMPLICIT MODELS:; URL: D:\SciHypoCheck\reports\openclaw_inputs\openclaw_input_20260608_072707.pdf; Confidence: 0.95
Recent research has sought to bridge the gap between implicit models, explicit deep networks, and kernel methods. (2022) extend the over-parameterization theory of explicit networks to implicit models, establishing well-posedness and convergence even in finite-width regimes where standard infinite-depth results do not directly apply. In the infinite-width limit, Feng & Kolter (2023) formally derive the Neural Tangent Kernel (NTK) for equilibrium models, characterizing their training dynamics in the linear regime.
Evidence ID: 52 (E-7c9bff41c54c); Source: input_pdf; Location: pdf:approach:p49; Paper: EXPRESSIVE POWER OF IMPLICIT MODELS:; URL: D:\SciHypoCheck\reports\openclaw_inputs\openclaw_input_20260608_072707.pdf; Confidence: 0.95
We prove that for a broad class of implicit models, this process allows the model’s expressive power to grow with test-time compute, ultimately matching a much richer function class. The theory is validated across four domains: imaging, scientific computing, operations research, and LLM reasoning, demonstrating that as test-time iterations increase, the complexity of the learned mapping rises, while the solution quality simultaneously improves and stabilizes.
Evidence ID: 53 (E-9552cf8c54fe); Source: input_pdf; Location: pdf:abstract:p1; Paper: EXPRESSIVE POWER OF IMPLICIT MODELS:; URL: D:\SciHypoCheck\reports\openclaw_inputs\openclaw_input_20260608_072707.pdf; Confidence: 0.95
(2022) extend the over-parameterization theory of explicit networks to implicit models, establishing well-posedness and convergence even in finite-width regimes where standard infinite-depth results do not directly apply. In the infinite-width limit, Feng & Kolter (2023) formally derive the Neural Tangent Kernel (NTK) for equilibrium models, characterizing their training dynamics in the linear regime. (2024) show that for high-dimensional Gaussian mixtures, deep equilibrium models can be functionally equivalent to shallow explicit networks.
Evidence ID: 54 (E-af5c889926d7); Source: input_pdf; Location: pdf:approach:p49; Paper: EXPRESSIVE POWER OF IMPLICIT MODELS:; URL: D:\SciHypoCheck\reports\openclaw_inputs\openclaw_input_20260608_072707.pdf; Confidence: 0.95
While it is empirically known that these compact models can often match or even exceed the accuracy of larger explicit networks by allocating more test-time compute, the underlying reasons are not yet well understood. We provide a strict mathematical characterization, showing that a simple and regular implicit operator can, through iteration, progressively express more complex mappings. We prove that for a broad class of implicit models, this process allows the model’s expressive power to grow with test-time compute, ultimately matching a much richer function class.
Evidence ID: 55 (E-8092c57e1c70); Source: input_pdf; Location: pdf:abstract:p1; Paper: EXPRESSIVE POWER OF IMPLICIT MODELS:; URL: D:\SciHypoCheck\reports\openclaw_inputs\openclaw_input_20260608_072707.pdf; Confidence: 0.95

Evidence ID: 45 (E-7acfc0965a3b); Source: arxiv; Location: pdf:introduction:p1; Paper: On the Neural Tangent Kernel of Equilibrium Models; URL: https://arxiv.org/pdf/2310.14062.pdf; Confidence: 0.946
We use n, d to denote width and depth respectively, and write G(d) n to stress G has depth d and width n, where G can represent either a kernel or a neural network. We write G(d) = limn→∞G(d) n , Gn = limd→∞G(d) n , and G = limn,d→∞G(d) n to denote limits are taken. 3 NTK-of-DEQ with Fully-connected Layers In this section, we show how to derive the NTK of the fully-connected DEQ. Define the depth-d approximation to a DEQ as the following: f (h) n (x) = r σ2 W n W (h)g(h−1)(x) + r σ2 U n U (h)x + r σ2 b n b(h), g(h) n (x) = σ(f (h)(x)), f (d+1) n (x) = σv · vT g(d)(x), where h ∈[d], W (h) ∈Rn×n, U (h) ∈Rn×m, v ∈Rn are the internal weights and b(h) ∈Rn are the bias terms. The forward pass is solved using root-finding or fixed point iteration, and the backward gradient is calculated using implicit function theorem instead of backpropogation.
Evidence ID: 56 (E-d396bfe7e90b); Source: arxiv; Location: pdf:background:p3; Paper: On the Neural Tangent Kernel of Equilibrium Models; URL: https://arxiv.org/pdf/2310.14062.pdf; Confidence: 0.878
We first show that unlike the infinite depth limit of NTK to FCNN, the DEQ-of-NTK does not converge to a degenerate kernel. This non-trivial kernel can be computed efficiently using root-finding. Although the proofs here involved infinite limits, we also show numerically that reasonably large networks converge to roughly the same quantities as predicted by theory, and we show the NTK-of-DEQ matches the performances of other NTKs on real-world datasets. 2.1 Notation We write capital letter W to represent matrices or tensors, which should be clear from the context, and use [W]i to represent the element of W indexed by i. We use n, d to denote width and depth respectively, and write G(d) n to stress G has depth d and width n, where G can represent either a kernel or a neural network.

Appendix C. Full Hypothesis and Claim Details

This appendix keeps the full hypothesis candidate list and atomic claim decomposition. The selected portfolio in Section 5 is the short answer; this section preserves lower-ranked candidates, selection roles, evidence IDs, scores, and the atomic claims derived from the selected hypothesis. It is useful for checking whether the final claim audit is evaluating the actual generated idea or only a simplified summary.

PrimaryMain candidate used for novelty search and claim audit.
IncrementalConservative fallback or A+B-style extension; useful but usually less novel.
Not selectedRetained for transparency; not the final answer.
Atomic claimA smaller claim derived from the selected hypothesis so citation support can be audited precisely.

Hypothesis Candidates with Scores

IDRoleHypothesisInference TypeEvidence IDsSelection ReasonScoresTotal
H1primaryThe neural tangent kernel (NTK) of an implicit model changes with the number of test-time fixed-point iterations, contradicting the infinite-width NTK theory that predicts a fixed kernel.inferred6(E-ef2c01b7d35a) 31(E-2a0765285b00) Primary proposal selected for the main novelty search and citation audit. It is the strongest non-incremental candidate when available; otherwise it is the highest-scoring distinct candidate.{'literature_support': 0.3, 'novelty': 0.8, 'feasibility': 0.7, 'cross_domain_value': 0.4, 'risk_penalty': 0.5, 'evidence_grounding': 0.6, 'mechanism_novelty': 0.9, 'surprisingness': 0.8, 'falsifiability': 0.9, 'closest_prior_similarity': 0.2, 'obvious_extension_penalty': 0.1}0.67
H2secondaryGeneralization bounds for implicit models that require contractivity can be extended to non-contractive regular operators by incorporating a Lipschitz constant that grows with the number of test-time iterations.inferred6(E-ef2c01b7d35a) 27(E-fb166f6208c3) Retained as the next strongest distinct hypothesis because no clear incremental candidate was available.{'literature_support': 0.4, 'novelty': 0.7, 'feasibility': 0.6, 'cross_domain_value': 0.3, 'risk_penalty': 0.4, 'evidence_grounding': 0.5, 'mechanism_novelty': 0.8, 'surprisingness': 0.6, 'falsifiability': 0.8, 'closest_prior_similarity': 0.3, 'obvious_extension_penalty': 0.2}0.505

Atomic Claims

  • CL1: P1 shows that the function class expressed by implicit models grows with iterations, while P5 claims the NTK is deterministic and fixed regardless of depth. If the NTK remains fixed, the increased expressivity must come from non-kernel aspects. However, it is possible that the NTK itself changes with iterations when the model is not in the infinite-width limit, or that the fixed-point iteration induces a different kernel. Testing this would bridge the gap between the two perspectives (G1, G3). (gap_motivation)
    Evidence IDs: 6(E-ef2c01b7d35a) 31(E-2a0765285b00)
  • CL2: This hypothesis challenges the prevailing NTK theory for implicit models by suggesting that the kernel is not static but evolves with test-time compute, which would reconcile the expressivity scaling with kernel-based analyses. (novelty_rationale)
    Evidence IDs: 6(E-ef2c01b7d35a) 31(E-2a0765285b00)
  • CL3: The neural tangent kernel (NTK) of an implicit model changes with the number of test-time fixed-point iterations, contradicting the infinite-width NTK theory that predicts a fixed kernel. (novel_claim_requires_experiment)
    Evidence IDs: 6(E-ef2c01b7d35a) 31(E-2a0765285b00)

Appendix D. Execution and Memory

This appendix shows execution details: the ordered tool trace, input/output summaries, raw memory summary, and memory update log. It is included to prove that the report came from a tool-calling pipeline with PDF parsing, retrieval, mapping, hypothesis generation, novelty search, verification, reflection, and persistence, rather than from a single prompt-only answer.

Ordered trace completeEvery tool call appears with step index, status, input summary, output summary, and latency.
Search before hypothesisRequired ordering constraint; if false, the run should not be accepted.
Tool errorA backend failed or returned unusable data; the pipeline may continue with fallback, cache, or other tools.
Memory update logShows what was persisted for future runs, including papers, hypotheses, evidence, and verification rows.

{'action': 'write_paper', 'profile': {'paper_id': 'aab57dc1f4dc', 'title': 'EXPRESSIVE POWER OF IMPLICIT MODELS:', 'authors': {'count': 3, 'items': ['Rich Equilibria', 'Test-Time Scaling', 'Jialin Liu']}, 'abstract': 'Implicit models, an emerging model class, compute outputs by iterating a sin- gle parameter block to a fixed point. This architecture realizes an infinite-depth, weight-tied network that trains with constant memory, significantly reducing memory needs for the same level of performance compared to ex...', 'sections': {'abstract': 'Implicit models, an emerging model class, compute outputs by iterating a sin- gle parameter block to a fixed point. This architecture realizes an infinite-depth, weight-tied network that trains with constant memory, significantly reducing memory needs for the same level of performance compared to ex...', 'introduction': 'Many machine-learning tasks can be cast as learning a mapping F from input x to the desired output y∗, i.e., y∗= F(x). An emerging alternative is the implicit models: train an operator G whose fixed point matches the target, i.e., y∗= G(y∗, x) (Bai et al., 2019; El Ghaoui et al., 2021). At inference...', 'appendix': 'CONTENTS A Proofs of Main Results 18 A.1 Proof of sufficiency . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . 18 A.2 Proof of necessity . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . 23 B A Variant Architecture 26 C Proofs of Theorems for Inverse Problems 2...'}}}

{'paper': {'title': 'EXPRESSIVE POWER OF IMPLICIT MODELS:', 'authors': {'count': 3, 'items': ['Rich Equilibria', 'Test-Time Scaling', 'Jialin Liu']}, 'year': None, 'venue': 'input PDF', 'doi': None}, 'claim': 'EXPRESSIVE POWER OF IMPLICIT MODELS: models implicit class test-time model'}
{'count': 6, 'items': [{'evidence_id': None, 'display_id': None, 'text': 'While it is empirically known that these compact models can often match or even exceed the accuracy of larger explicit networks by allocating more test-time compute, the underlying reasons are not yet well understood. We provide a strict mathematical characterization, showing that a simple and regul...', 'source': 'input_pdf', 'location': 'pdf:abstract:p1'}, {'evidence_id': None, 'display_id': None, 'text': 'While universality has been touched upon in specific settings (Bai et al., 2019; Marwah et al., 2023) and separation results have demonstrated advantages over explicit models (Wu et al., 2024), a complete characterization of the representable function class of implicit models (and hence a direct ans...', 'source': 'input_pdf', 'location': 'pdf:introduction:p2'}, {'evidence_id': None, 'display_id': None, 'text': 'In particular, can a relatively simple implicit operator G, through iteration, represent a complex explicit map F? While universality has been touched upon in specific settings (Bai et al., 2019; Marwah et al., 2023) and separation results have demonstrated advantages over explicit models (Wu et al....', 'source': 'input_pdf', 'location': 'pdf:introduction:p2'}, {'evidence_id': None, 'display_id': None, 'text': 'We prove that for a broad class of implicit models, this process allows the model’s expressive power to grow with test-time compute, ultimately matching a much richer function class. {'paper': {'title': 'Deep Equilibrium Models are Almost Equivalent to Not-so-deep Explicit Models for High-dimensional Gaussian Mixtures', 'authors': {'count': 7, 'items': ['Zenan Ling', 'Longbo Li', 'Zhanbo Feng', 'Yixuan Zhang', 'Feng Zhou']}, 'year': 2024, 'venue': 'CoRR 2024', 'doi': None}, 'claim': 'EXPRESSIVE POWER OF IMPLICIT MODELS: models implicit class test-time model'}
{'count': 6, 'items': [{'evidence_id': None, 'display_id': None, 'text': 'We prove, in this setting, that the spectral behavior of these Implicit-CKs and NTKs depend on the DEQ activation function and initial weight variances, but only via a system of four nonlinear equations.', 'source': 'openreview', 'location': 'abstract'}, {'evidence_id': None, 'display_id': None, 'text': 'In this paper, leveraging recent advances in random matrix theory (RMT), we perform an in-depth analysis on the eigenspectra of the conjugate kernel (CK) and neural tangent kernel (NTK) matrices for implicit DEQs, when the input data are drawn from a high-dimensional Gaussian mixture.', 'source': 'openreview', 'location': 'abstract'}, {'evidence_id': None, 'display_id': None, 'text': 'Deep equilibrium models (DEQs), as a typical implicit neural network, have demonstrated remarkable success on various tasks.', 'source': 'openreview', 'location': 'abstract'}, {'evidence_id': None, 'display_id': None, 'text': 'There is, however, a lack of theoretical understanding of the connections and differences between implicit DEQs and explicit neural network models.', 'source': 'openreview', 'location': 'abstract'}, {'evidence_id': None, 'display_id': None, 'text': 'As a direct consequence of this theoretical result, we demonstrate that a shallow explicit network can be carefully designed to produce the same CK or NTK as a given DEQ.', 'source': 'openreview', 'location': 'abstract'}]}
211evidence_fetcherok4041
{'paper': {'title': 'A Generalization Bound for a Family of Implicit Networks', 'authors': {'count': 2, 'items': ['Samy Wu Fung', 'Benjamin Berkels']}, 'year': 2024, 'venue': 'CoRR 2024', 'doi': None}, 'claim': 'EXPRESSIVE POWER OF IMPLICIT MODELS: models implicit class test-time model'}
{'count': 5, 'items': [{'evidence_id': None, 'display_id': None, 'text': 'Implicit networks are a class of neural networks whose outputs are defined by the fixed point of a parameterized operator.', 'source': 'openreview', 'location': 'abstract'}, {'evidence_id': None, 'display_id': None, 'text': 'In this work, we consider a large family of implicit networks defined parameterized contractive fixed point operators.', 'source': 'openreview', 'location': 'abstract'}, {'evidence_id': None, 'display_id': None, 'text': 'We show a generalization bound for this class based on a covering number argument for the Rademacher complexity of these architectures.', 'source': 'openreview', 'location': 'abstract'}, {'evidence_id': None, 'display_id': None, 'text': 'They have enjoyed success in many applications including natural language processing, image processing, and numerous other applications.', 'source': 'openreview', 'location': 'abstract'}, {'evidence_id': None, 'display_id': None, 'text': 'While they have found abundant empirical success, theoretical work on its generalization is still under-explored.', 'source': 'openreview', 'location': 'abstract'}]}

{'paper': {'title': 'On the Neural Tangent Kernel of Equilibrium Models', 'authors': {'count': 2, 'items': ['Zhili Feng', 'J. Zico Kolter']}, 'year': 2023, 'venue': 'arXiv', 'doi': None}, 'claim': 'EXPRESSIVE POWER OF IMPLICIT MODELS: models implicit class test-time model'}
{'count': 6, 'items': [{'evidence_id': None, 'display_id': None, 'text': 'This work studies the neural tangent kernel (NTK) of the deep equilibrium (DEQ) model, a practical “infinite-depth” architecture which directly computes the infinite-depth limit of a weight-tied network via root-finding. Even though the NTK of a fully-connected neural network can be stochastic if it...', 'source': 'arxiv', 'location': 'pdf:abstract:p1'}, {'evidence_id': None, 'display_id': None, 'text': 'Even though the NTK of a fully-connected neural network can be stochastic if its width and depth both tend to infinity simultaneously, we show that contrarily a DEQ model still enjoys a deterministic NTK despite its width and depth going to infinity at the same time under mild conditions. Moreover, ...', 'source': 'arxiv', 'location': 'pdf:abstract:p1'}, {'evidence_id': None, 'display_id': None, 'text': 'Implicit models form a new class of machine learning models where instead of stacking explicit “layers”, they output z s.t g(x, z) = 0, where g can be either a fixed point equation (Bai et al., 2019), a differential equation (Chen et al., 2018b), or an optimization problem (Gould et al., 2019). This...', 'source': 'arxiv', 'location': 'pdf:introduction:p1'}, {'evidence_id': None, 'display_id': None, 'text': 'A weight-tied FCNN with input injection (FCNN-IJ) makes the bias term related to the original input and ties the weight in each layer by taking the form z(t) := f(z(t−1), x) = σ(Wz(t−1) + Ux + b). {'count': 6, 'items': [{'evidence_id': None, 'display_id': None, 'text': 'Deep neural networks are notorious for being sensitive to small well-chosen perturbations, and estimating the regularity of such architectures is of utmost importance for safe and robust practical applications. Our experiments show that SeqLip can signiﬁcantly improve on the existing upper bounds. Finally, we prov...', 'source': 'arxiv', 'location': 'pdf:method:p1'}, {'evidence_id': None, 'display_id': None, 'text': 'We provide a power method algorithm working with automatic differentiation, allowing efficient computations even on large convolutions.', 'source': 'arxiv', 'location': 'abstract'}, {'evidence_id': None, 'display_id': None, 'text': 'First, we show that, even for two layer neural networks, the exact computation of this quantity is NP-hard and state-of-art methods may significantly overestimate it.', 'source': 'arxiv', 'location': 'abstract'}]}

{'action': 'write_evidence_records', 'records': {'count': 42, 'items': [{'evidence_id': 'E-104d26f8718c', 'display_id': '1', 'source_type': 'input_pdf', 'source_name': 'D:\\SciHypoCheck\\reports\\openclaw_inputs\\openclaw_input_20260608_072707.pdf', 'text': 'Implicit models, an emerging model class, compute outputs by iterating a sin- gle parameter block to a fixed point.'}, {'evidence_id': 'E-6b38440bdb02', 'display_id': '2', 'source_type': 'input_pdf', 'source_name': 'D:\\SciHypoCheck\\reports\\openclaw_inputs\\openclaw_input_20260608_072707.pdf', 'text': 'This architecture realizes an infinite-depth, weight-tied network that trains with constant memory, significantly reducing memory needs for the same level of performance compared to explicit models.'}, {'evidence_id': 'E-c14e24913c5c', 'display_id': '3', 'source_type': 'input_pdf', 'source_name': 'D:\\SciHypoCheck\\reports\\openclaw_inputs\\openclaw_input_20260608_072707.pdf', 'text': 'While it is empirically known that these compact models can often match or even exceed the accuracy of larger explicit networks by allocating more test-time com- pute, the underlying reasons are not yet well understood.'}, {'evidence_id': 'E-a8456cb46351', 'display_id': '4', 'source_type': 'input_pdf', 'source_name': 'D:\\SciHypoCheck\\reports\\openclaw_inputs\\openclaw_input_20260608_072707.pdf', 'text': 'We study this gap through a non-parametric analysis of expressive power.'}, {'evidence_id': 'E-0f6dd8e0ef31', 'display_id': '5', 'source_type': 'input_pdf', 'source_name': 'D:\\SciHypoCheck\\reports\\openclaw_inputs\\openclaw_input_20260608_072707.pdf', 'text': 'We provide a strict mathematical characterization, showing that a simple and regular implicit operator can, through iteration, progressively express more complex map- pings.'}]}}

test-time compute (iterations) (E-ef2c01b7d35a, E-9889a37ce32f).", 'The fixed points of regular implicit operators exactly represent the class of locally Lipschitz maps (E-f6d21ba2c553, E-7f2ca99a93d9).', 'Empirically, as iterations increase, mapping complexity rises and solution quality improves and stabilizes (E-a4d44403a92f, E-430566fe09ad).']}}, {'thread_id': 'T2', 'name': 'Equivalence between implicit DEQs and shallow explicit networks under Gaussian mixtures', 'description': 'P3 (and P6) shows that for high-dimensional Gaussian mixture data, the conjugate kernel and neural tangent kernel of a DEQ depend only on a system of four nonlinear equations, and a shallow explicit network can be designed to produce the same kernel, implying functional equivalence in that setting.', 'representative_papers': {'count': 2, 'items': [{'title': 'Deep Equilibrium Models are Almost Equivalent to Not-so-deep Explicit Models for High-dimensional Gaussian Mixtures', 'authors': {'count': 7, 'items': ['Zenan Ling', 'Longbo Li', 'Zhanbo Feng', 'Yixuan Zhang', 'Feng Zhou']}, 'year': 2024, 'venue': 'CoRR 2024', 'doi': None}, {'title': 'Deep Equilibrium Models are almost equivalent to not-so-deep explicit models for highdimensional Gaussian mixtures', 'authors': {'count': 6, 'items': ['Zenan Ling', 'Longbo Li', 'Zhanbo Feng', 'Yixuan Zhang', 'Feng Zhou']}, 'year': (E-4c9c96d76c49).', "A shallow explicit network can match the DEQ's CK/NTK (E-527e23c5f37e).", 'This equivalence extends to real-world datasets empirically (E-6b9471f9dbd9).']}}, {'thread_id': 'T3', 'name': 'Generalization bounds for contractive implicit networks', 'description': 'P4 provides a generalization bound for implicit networks defined by contractive fixed-point operators, using Rademacher complexity and covering number arguments, addressing the under-explored theoretical generalization of implicit models.', 'representative_papers': {'count': 1, 'items': [{'title': 'A Generalization Bound for a Family of Implicit Networks', 'authors': {'count': 2, 'items': ['Samy Wu Fung', 'Benjamin Berkels']}, 'year': 2024, 'venue': 'CoRR 2024', 'doi': None}]}, 'main_claims': implicit networks is still under-explored (E-7a88263eecd8).']}}, {'thread_id': 'T4', 'name': 'Deterministic NTK of deep equilibrium models', 'description': 'P5 shows that DEQ models have a deterministic NTK even when width and depth go to infinity simultaneously, unlike explicit networks, and this NTK can be computed efficiently via root-finding.', 'representative_papers': {'count': 1, 'items': [{'title': 'On the Neural Tangent Kernel of Equilibrium Models', 'authors': {'count': 2, 'items': ['Zhili Feng', 'J. Zico Kolter']}, 'year': 2023, 'venue': 'arXiv', 'doi': None}]}, 'main_claims': {'count': 2, 'items': ['DEQ models enjoy a deterministic NTK in the infinite width and depth limit (E-2a0765285b00).', 'The deterministic NTK can be found efficiently via root-finding (E-69f5faf264a4).']}}]}, 'gaps': {'count': 4, 'items': [{'gap_id': 'G1', 'description': "The connection between the expressive power scaling with test-time compute (P1) and the kernel-based equivalence to shallow explicit networks (P3) is not established. We ident...', 'source': 'input_pdf', 'location': 'pdf:introduction:p2'}]}, 'supporting_evidence_ids': {'count': 2, 'items': ['E-0f6dd8e0ef31', 'E-f6d21ba2c553']}}]}, 'tensions': {'count': 3, 'items': [{'tension_id': 'X1', 'description': 'P1 claims that implicit models can match or exceed explicit model accuracy by using more test-time compute, while P3 suggests that DEQs are almost equivalent to shallow explicit networks in terms of kernel behavior for Gaussian mixtures. {'profiles': {'count': 1, 'items': [{'paper_id': 'aab57dc1f4dc', 'title': 'EXPRESSIVE POWER OF IMPLICIT MODELS:', 'authors': {'count': 3, 'items': ['Rich Equilibria', 'Test-Time Scaling', 'Jialin Liu']}, 'abstract': 'Implicit models, an emerging model class, compute outputs by iterating a sin- gle parameter block to a fixed point. This architecture realizes an infinite-depth, weight-tied network that trains with constant memory, significantly reducing memory needs for the same level of performance compared to ex...', 'sections': {'abstract': 'Implicit models, an emerging model class, compute outputs by iterating a sin- gle parameter block to a fixed point. This architecture realizes an infinite-depth, weight-tied network that trains with constant memory, significantly reducing memory needs for the same level of performance compared to ex...', 'introduction': 'Many machine-learning tasks can be cast as learning a mapping F from input x to the desired output y∗, i.e., y∗= F(x). An emerging alternative is the implicit models: train an operator G whose fixed point matches the target, i.e., y∗= G(y∗, x) (Bai et al., 2019; El Ghaoui et al., 2021). At inference...', 'appendix': 'CONTENTS A Proofs of Main Results 18 A.1 Proof of sufficiency . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . 18 A.2 Proof of necessity . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . 23 B A Variant Architecture 26 C Proofs of Theorems for Inverse Problems 2...'}}]}, 'literature_map': {'threads': {'count': 4, 'items': [{'thread_id': 'T1', 'name': 'Expressive power scaling with test-time compute in implicit models', 'description': 'P1 and P2 establish that implicit models can match or exceed explicit model accuracy by allocating more test-time compute, and provide a non-parametric analysis showing that expressive power grows with iterations, ultimately representing locally Lipschitz functions. We ident...', 'source': 'input_pdf', 'location': 'pdf:introduction:p2'}]}, 'supporting_evidence_ids': {'count': 2, 'items': ['E-0f6dd8e0ef31', 'E-f6d21ba2c553']}}]}, 'tensions': {'count': 3, 'items': [{'tension_id': 'X1', 'description': 'P1 claims that implicit models can match or exceed explicit model accuracy by using more test-time compute, while P3 suggests that DEQs are almost equivalent to shallow explicit networks in terms of kernel behavior for Gaussian mixtures. This architecture realizes an infinite-depth, weight-tied network that trains with constant memory, significantly reducing memory needs for the same level of performance compared to ex...', 'sections': {'abstract': 'Implicit models, an emerging model class, compute outputs by iterating a sin- gle parameter block to a fixed point. This architecture realizes an infinite-depth, weight-tied network that trains with constant memory, significantly reducing memory needs for the same level of performance compared to ex...', 'introduction': 'Many machine-learning tasks can be cast as learning a mapping F from input x to the desired output y∗, i.e., y∗= F(x). An emerging alternative is the implicit models: train an operator G whose fixed point matches the target, i.e., y∗= G(y∗, x) (Bai et al., 2019; El Ghaoui et al., 2021). 'EXPRESSIVE POWER OF IMPLICIT MODELS:', 'url': None, 'pdf_path': 'D:\\SciHypoCheck\\reports\\openclaw_inputs\\openclaw_input_20260608_072707.pdf', 'page': 1, 'section': 'abstract', 'location': 'abstract sentence 7', 'confidence': 1.0}, {'evidence_id': 'E-53bf17e86efc', 'display_id': '8', 'source_type': 'full_text_pdf', 'source_name': 'input_pdf', 'text': 'While it is empirically known that these compact models can often match or even exceed the accuracy of larger explicit networks by allocating more test-time compute, the underlying reasons are not yet well understood. We provide a strict mathematical characterization, showing that a simple and regular implicit operator can, through iteration, progressively express more complex mappings. We prove that for a broad class of implicit models, this process allows the model’s expressive power to grow with test-time compute, ultimately matching a much richer function class.', 'paper_title': 'EXPRESSIVE POWER OF IMPLICIT MODELS:', 'url': None, 'pdf_path': 'D:\\SciHypoCheck\\reports\\openclaw_inputs\\openclaw_input_20260608_072707.pdf', 'page': None, 'section': 'pdf:abstract:p1', 'location': 'pdf:abstract:p1', 'confidence': 0.95}, {'evidence_id': 'E-2406f45fde75', 'display_id': '9', 'source_type': 'full_text_pdf', 'source_name': 'input_pdf', 'text': 'While universality has been touched upon in specific settings (Bai et al., 2019; Marwah et al., 2023) and separation results have demonstrated advantages over explicit models (Wu et al., 2024), a complete characterization of the representable function class of implicit models (and hence a direct answer to questions (Q1) and (Q2)) is still missing. Unlike studies focusing on infinite-width limits and kernel connections (Gao et al., 2022; Feng & Kolter, 2023; Ling et al., 2024), our work fills this gap from a nonparametric, function-space perspective, establishing that an implicit model’s expressive power scales with test-time compute. We identify locally Lipschitz mappings as a natural target class and prove: every such map F can be expressed as the fixed point of a “regular” (simple and wellbehaved) G, and conversely, every such fixed-point map is locally Lipschitz.', 'paper_title': 'EXPRESSIVE POWER OF IMPLICIT MODELS:', 'url': None, 'pdf_path': 'D:\\SciHypoCheck\\reports\\openclaw_inputs\\openclaw_input_20260608_072707.pdf', 'page': None, 'section': 'pdf:introduction:p2', 'location': 'pdf:introduction:p2', 'confidence': 0.95}, {'evidence_id': 'E-94adf4a12de7', 'display_id': '10', 'source_type': 'full_text_pdf', 'source_name': 'input_pdf', 'text': 'In particular, can a relatively simple implicit operator G, through iteration, represent a complex explicit map F? While universality has been touched upon in specific settings (Bai et al., 2019; Marwah et al., 2023) and separation results have demonstrated advantages over explicit models (Wu et al., 2024), a complete characterization of the representable function class of implicit models (and hence a direct answer to questions (Q1) and (Q2)) is still missing. Unlike studies focusing on infinite-width limits and kernel connections (Gao et al., 2022; Feng & Kolter, 2023; Ling et al., 2024), our work fills this gap from a nonparametric, function-space perspective, establishing that an implicit model’s expressive power scales with test-time compute.', 'paper_title': 'EXPRESSIVE POWER OF IMPLICIT MODELS:', 'url': None, 'pdf_path': 'D:\\SciHypoCheck\\reports\\openclaw_inputs\\openclaw_input_20260608_072707.pdf', 'page': None, 'section': 'pdf:introduction:p2', 'location': 'pdf:introduction:p2', 'confidence': 0.95}, {'evidence_id': 'E-e75831b345d0', 'display_id': '11', 'source_type': 'full_text_pdf', 'source_name': 'input_pdf', 'text': 'We prove that for a broad class of implicit models, this process allows the model’s expressive power to grow with test-time compute, ultimately matching a much richer function class. The theory is validated across four domains: imaging, scientific computing, operations research, and LLM reasoning, demonstrating that as test-time iterations increase, the complexity of the learned mapping rises, while the solution quality simultaneously improves and stabilizes.', 'paper_title': 'EXPRESSIVE POWER OF IMPLICIT MODELS:', 'url': None, 'pdf_path': 'D:\\SciHypoCheck\\reports\\openclaw_inputs\\openclaw_input_20260608_072707.pdf', 'page': None, 'section': 'pdf:abstract:p1', 'location': 'pdf:abstract:p1', 'confidence': 0.95}, {'evidence_id': 'E-f6d21ba2c553', 'display_id': '12', 'source_type': 'full_text_pdf', 'source_name': 'input_pdf', 'text': 'Unlike studies focusing on infinite-width limits and kernel connections (Gao et al., 2022; Feng & Kolter, 2023; Ling et al., 2024), our work fills this gap from a nonparametric, function-space perspective, establishing that an implicit model’s expressive power scales with test-time compute. We identify locally Lipschitz mappings as a natural target class and prove: every such map F can be expressed as the fixed point of a “regular” (simple and wellbehaved) G, and conversely, every such fixed-point map is locally Lipschitz. Our theory, combined with iterative solvers’ dynamics, yields a new viewpoint on implicit models: the expressive power is progressively unlocked by iterations.', 'paper_title': 'EXPRESSIVE POWER OF IMPLICIT MODELS:', 'url': None, 'pdf_path': 'D:\\SciHypoCheck\\reports\\openclaw_inputs\\openclaw_input_20260608_072707.pdf', 'page': None, 'section': 'pdf:introduction:p2', 'location': 'pdf:introduction:p2', 'confidence': 0.95}, {'evidence_id': 'E-f261d552c740', 'display_id': '13', 'source_type': 'full_text_pdf', 'source_name': 'input_pdf', 'text': 'Our theory, combined with iterative solvers’ dynamics, yields a new viewpoint on implicit models: the expressive power is progressively unlocked by iterations. We validate our theory with case studies in a wide range of applications (e.g., image reconstruction, scientific computing, operations research, and LLM reasoning). Note that, while explicit networks are capable of expressing locally Lipschitz target maps (Beneventano et al., 2021) by scaling up the model size, implicit models can scale expressivity with test-time compute (i.e., more iterations at inference), which scales up test-time runtime but not parameters. This prompts the natural follow-up: is there any nontrivial implicit representation that is able to indicate the expressive benefits of implicit models? Neural networks approximating 1/x on [−1, −δ) ∪(δ, 1] typically demands higher network complexity—i.e., increasing depth/width as δ →0 to capture the growing steepness near the singularity (Telgarsky, 2017).', 'paper_title': 'EXPRESSIVE POWER OF IMPLICIT MODELS:', 'url': None, 'pdf_path': 'D:\\SciHypoCheck\\reports\\openclaw_inputs\\openclaw_input_20260608_072707.pdf', 'page': None, 'section': 'pdf:introduction:p2', 'location': 'pdf:introduction:p2', 'confidence': 0.95}, {'evidence_id': 'E-e7a12e5282d2', 'display_id': '14', 'source_type': 'full_text_pdf', 'source_name': 'arxiv', 'text': 'While universality has been touched upon in specific settings [3, 73] and separation results have demonstrated advantages over explicit models [106], a complete characterization of the representable function class of implicit models (and hence a direct answer to questions (Q1) and (Q2)) is still missing. Unlike studies focusing on infinite-width limits and kernel connections [29,33,63], our work fills this gap from a nonparametric, function-space perspective, establishing that an implicit model’s expressive power scales with test-time compute.', 'paper_title': 'Expressive Power of Implicit Models: Rich Equilibria and Test-Time Scaling', 'url': 'https://arxiv.org/pdf/2510.03638', 'pdf_path': None, 'page': None, 'section': 'pdf:results:p2', 'location': 'pdf:results:p2', 'confidence': 0.95}, {'evidence_id': 'E-9889a37ce32f', 'display_id': '15', 'source_type': 'full_text_pdf', 'source_name': 'arxiv', 'text': 'While it is empirically known that these compact models can often match or even exceed the accuracy of larger explicit networks by allocating more test-time compute, the underlying mechanism remains poorly understood. We provide a strict mathematical characterization, showing that a simple and regular implicit operator can, through iteration, progressively express more complex mappings. We prove that for a broad class of implicit models, this process lets the model’s expressive power scale with test-time compute, ultimately matching a much richer function class.', 'paper_title': 'Expressive Power of Implicit Models: Rich Equilibria and Test-Time Scaling', 'url': 'https://arxiv.org/pdf/2510.03638', 'pdf_path': None, 'page': None, 'section': 'pdf:abstract:p1', 'location': 'pdf:abstract:p1', 'confidence': 0.95}, {'evidence_id': 'E-430566fe09ad', 'display_id': '16', 'source_type': 'full_text_pdf', 'source_name': 'arxiv', 'text': 'We prove that for a broad class of implicit models, this process lets the model’s expressive power scale with test-time compute, ultimately matching a much richer function class. The theory is validated across four domains: image reconstruction, scientific computing, operations research, and LLM reasoning, demonstrating that as test-time iterations increase, the complexity of the learned mapping rises, while the solution quality simultaneously improves and stabilizes.', 'paper_title': 'Expressive Power of Implicit Models: Rich Equilibria and Test-Time Scaling', 'url': 'https://arxiv.org/pdf/2510.03638', 'pdf_path': None, 'page': None, 'section': 'pdf:abstract:p1', 'location': 'pdf:abstract:p1', 'confidence': 0.95}, {'evidence_id': 'E-063264a54711', 'display_id': '17', 'source_type': 'full_text_pdf', 'source_name': 'arxiv', 'text': 'While our work primarily establishes the expressive power of implicit models through the lens of fixed-point iterations, we situate our contributions within the broader landscape of implicit model theory in this section. For example, while [3] demonstrated that an operator g exists such that its fixed point reproduces any explicit network f, this existence result crucially does not guarantee that the fixed-point iteration (1) actually converges. Other works have established universality within restricted domains, such as steady-state PDEs [73], or proven separation results where implicit models outperform explicit counterparts in specific settings [106]. While insightful, none of these studies provide a complete characterization of the general function class representable by implicit models, nor do they directly address the fundamental questions (Q1) and (Q2) raised in our introduction.', 'paper_title': 'Expressive Power of Implicit Models: Rich Equilibria and Test-Time Scaling', 'url': 'https://arxiv.org/pdf/2510.03638', 'pdf_path': None, 'page': None, 'section': 'pdf:appendix:p47', 'location': 'pdf:appendix:p47', 'confidence': 0.95}, {'evidence_id': 'E-774bba145295', 'display_id': '18', 'source_type': 'full_text_pdf', 'source_name': 'arxiv', 'text': 'Our theory, combined with the root-finding solvers’ dynamics, yields a new viewpoint on implicit models: the expressive power of a regular implicit operator is not static but progressively unlocked by iterations (i.e., scales with test-time compute), and finally matches a much richer function class. We validate our theory with case studies in a wide range of applications (e.g., image reconstruction, scientific computing, operations research, and LLM reasoning). For a representative problem in each domain, we demonstrating that as test-time iterations increase, the empirical complexity of the iterate yt(x) rises while solution quality improves and stabilizes.', 'paper_title': 'Expressive Power of Implicit Models: Rich Equilibria and Test-Time Scaling', 'url': 'https://arxiv.org/pdf/2510.03638', 'pdf_path': None, 'page': None, 'section': 'pdf:appendix:p2', 'location': 'pdf:appendix:p2', 'confidence': 0.95}, {'evidence_id': 'E-7f2ca99a93d9', 'display_id': '19', 'source_type': 'full_text_pdf', 'source_name': 'arxiv', 'text': 'Consistent with our theory, this growth reflects the model’s emergent capacity to map proximal inputs to semantically distinct outputs. Even in the discrete domain of language reasoning, iterating a fixed operator allows the model to scale its expressive power, evolving from simple surface-level processing to complex, context-aware reasoning. Conclusions We establish a sharp expressivity boundary for regular implicit models: as iterations increase, their expressive power grows, and the resulting fixed points can represent exactly the class of locally Lipschitz maps.', 'paper_title': 'Expressive Power of Implicit Models: Rich Equilibria and Test-Time Scaling', 'url': 'https://arxiv.org/pdf/2510.03638', 'pdf_path': None, 'page': None,
